Architecture for Customization-Based ATSaaS Platform Figure 3 illustrates a comprehensive architecture of a customization-based ATSaaS platform, demonstrating how user-driven customization enables precise control over avia- tion maintenance processes. This system architecture comprises four primary layers: User Interface Layer, Process Management, Service Processing, and Data Management. Processes 2025, 13, 666 18 of 54 Figure 3. Data flow architecture for customization-based ATSaaS platform. The User Interface Layer forms the foundation of user interaction, where the Air- line Maintenance Team directly engages with the system. Through the Configuration Dashboard, users can access Service Parameter Forms to input specific maintenance re- quirements. The Customization Options component allows for detailed specification of maintenance parameters, while the Validation Interface ensures the accuracy and feasibility of user inputs before they are processed further. Processes 2025, 13, x FOR PEER REVIEW 17 of 53 Figure 3. Data flow architecture for customization-based ATSaaS platform. The User Interface Layer forms the foundation of user interaction, where the Airline Maintenance Team directly engages with the system. Through the Configuration Dash-board, users can access Service Parameter Forms to input specific maintenance require-ments. The Customization Options component allows for detailed specification of mainte-nance parameters, while the Validation Interface ensures the accuracy and feasibility of user inputs before they are processed further. The Process Management layer implements the business logic necessary to transform user requirements into actionable maintenance plans. The Rule-based Workflow Engine processes user inputs according to predefined business rules and industry standards. Configuration Management ensures proper organization of user specifications, while Processes 2025, 13, 666 19 of 54 The Process Management layer implements the business logic necessary to transform user requirements into actionable maintenance plans. The Rule-based Workflow Engine processes user inputs according to predefined business rules and industry standards. Configuration Management ensures proper organization of user specifications, while Parameter Validation provides an additional layer of verification. The Service Module Selection component then determines the appropriate maintenance modules based on validated parameters. In the Service Processing layer, the system converts validated configurations into specific maintenance actions. Modular Service Components provide the building blocks for customized maintenance solutions, which are then refined through Task-Specific Adjust- ments. Custom Inspection Rules are generated based on user specifications, and finally, the Maintenance Schedule Generator creates a comprehensive maintenance timeline tailored to the airline’s needs. The Data Management layer serves as the system’s memory and audit mechanism. The User Input Database

input.query: Wie ist der aktuelle Entwicklungsstand von Technologie Radio Block Centre (RBC)
input.research_plan_reasoning: Die Frage zielt auf den Stand der Technik von Radio Block Centre (RBC) im Kontext ETCS/ERTMS (Schienenverkehr). Dafür sind sowohl normative Grundlagen (Baselines/Spezifikationen), Implementierungs-/Produktstände (Lieferanten, Architekturen), Sicherheitsnachweise (SIL, CENELEC) sowie Migrations-/Zukunftsthemen (FRMCS, Cloud/virtualisierte RBC, ATO) relevant.

research_aspects.description: Ermittlung des Entwicklungsstands anhand aktueller SRS/Baselines (z.B. Subset-026) und der RBC-relevanten Interfaces/Telegramme (u.a. Subset-039/098) als Referenz für Anforderungen und Kompatibilität.

Analyse des Standes der Technik bei Architekturvarianten (klassisch, zentralisiert, verteilt, virtualisiert/cloud) inklusive Skalierbarkeit, Verfügbarkeit, Kapazität (Zuganzahl), Latenz und Interoperabilität.
Vergleich aktueller RBC-Produktgenerationen und Implementierungsreife bei Hauptanbietern (Siemens, Alstom, Hitachi, Thales etc.), inkl. Referenzprojekte, Release-Stände und Roadmaps.
Bewertung des Entwicklungsstands über Safety-Lifecycle-Reife: SIL4-Nachweise, Anwendung EN 50126/50128/50129, Safety Case, unabhängige Bewertung (ISA/AsBo) und Zulassungsansätze.
Einordnung des RBC-Entwicklungsstands im Übergang von GSM-R zu FRMCS, Integration mit ATO/CTMS, sowie Umsetzung von Cybersecurity-Anforderungen (z.B. TS 50701/IEC 62443) und Betriebsmodellen.

content.title: Modeling of RAILWAY LogicS for Reverse Engineering, Verification and Refactoring
content.abstract_or_snippet: Interlocking (IXl) system, which is not standardized in ERTMS. Moreover, the RBc is usually able to manage a limited number of track sections, thus it must be connected to other adjacent RBcs in order to allow for the so-called train Hand-Over (HO). The Hand-Over is the complex procedure that manages the passage of a train from the area supervised by a RBc to the area of adjacent RBcs. In ERTMS level 2, the on-board and track- side communicate by the gSM-R radio network using the Euroradio protocol (see Fig. 3). The software architecture of the RBc is depicted in Fig. 3. It consists of some ‘application’ processes (written in a safe subset of the c programming language), which manage the inter- action of the RBc with the external entities (i.e. other subsystems and the RBc operator), and some ‘logic’ processes, which implement the control logic of the RBc; the latter are written in an application specific logic language, as already mentioned above. The logic Manager has to interpret logic language, translating it into executable code, and to schedule the logic processes. The shaded rectangles in Fig. 4 represent the logic processes, which are the target of our work. For instance, the ‘MA’ process, shown in Fig. 3, verifies the integrity of the Movement Authority assigned to the train against all the significant track and route conditions. Such conditions are received from the interlocking system by means of the ‘IXl interaction’ application process and are managed by the ‘Tc’ logic process, which stores Track circuit physical conditions in its internal variables. If the MA integrity verifica- tion fails (for instance a track circuit is not clear or involved in an emergency condition), the 86 F. Flammini, et al., Int. MA process has to command the sending of an emergency message to the ‘Train interaction’ application process, which will manage the sending of the proper radio message to the train. content.abstract_or_snippet: the architecture of the typical CBTC system is in- troduced, and the characteristics of CBTC systems are summarized which are much different from the other ICS. Therefore, the security issue of CBTC systems are present. 2.1. A typical CBTC system The aim of CBTC is to improve the efficiency of train operation through the application of wireless communications, and the level of system safety should also be enhanced. Fig. 1 describes a typical CBTC system, which consists of on-board subsystems, Data Communication Systems (DCS) and trackside subsystems. The trackside subsystems are connected through the wired (Ethernet) network, which provide the movement. The Movement Authority (MA) for on-board systems through the wireless network. Similarly, the information of trains including the current location and the speed is transmitted to the trackside subsystems which helps to calculate the MA. Although the same system architecture is ap- plied, the high speed railway systems are much more complicated than the urban rail transit systems and some distinctions are shown in Table 1. In the view of functions, the VOBC includes Automatic Train Operation (ATO) and Automatic Train Protection (ATP), but there is no ATO for high speed railways. For trackside subsystems, the RBC and the ZC almost plays the same role, which is to generate and transmit the MA information. One big difference is the main method of train-ground wireless communica- tions, where the high speed railway is using GSM-R and the urban rail transit is using WLAN or other COTS technologies. The railway line is usually divided into areas or regions. Each area is under the control of a ZC or RBC and has its own wireless transmission Fig. 1. content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: [22]. Specifi- cally, it shows different features when the CBTC technologies are used respectively in high-speed railways and urban rail transit systems. The high-speed railway systems apply GSM-R as the primary method of train-ground commu- nications while the WLANs are adopted in urban rail transit systems. According to the analysis on GSM-R [23], there is no end to end encryption in GSM-R protocols, which means the wireless link between Mobile Stations (MS) and Base Stations (BS) is encrypted while the information between BS and BS is transmitted in clear texts. What’s more, the GSM- R uses one-way authentication, which means the network (BS) needs to authenticate the MS when it tries to access but there is no the same authentication process when the BS tries to communication with the MS. As a result, some active attacks can effi- ciently steal information transmitted in the GSM-R network in the form of false BS and make the network collapse. As WLANs work at the public frequency band, the urban rail transit systems face the same security risks compared with high-speed railway systems. Firstly, the interference is unavoidable, which has attracted the attention of subway operators and some research has been done [24–26]. Secondly, as the 802.11 protocols are not originally designed for railways, it shows some problems when WLANs are applied as the main method of train-ground communications, such as the handoff mechanisms [21]. Above all, the working frequency of WLANs is high and the coverage of one AP is small, which is 300500 m. As a result, the handoff happens frequently when the train travels with the high speed, and it provides more opportunities for attackers to access the network with the deficiency of WLAN handoff mechanisms. In fact, there are various WLAN products of different manufactures in urban rail transit systems, including Cisco, Simens, H3C and so on. Because of the im- perfection of 802.11 protocols, there is big difference between different products and it brings much more security issues for CBTC systems. However, as the CBTC systems could also be considered as a typical cyber physical system [27], the CBTC system could be divided into two parts: cyber space and physical space. According to the ANSI/ISA 99 standards, a reference model for ICS systems is proposed, and we build the same one based on the architecture of CBTC systems as shown in Fig. 2. content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: Other COTS Technologies system. Each train transmits its identity (ID), position, direction and ve- locity to the ZC (RBC). The continuous wireless link between each train and the ZC (RBC) can ensure that the ZC (RBC) knows the status in- formation of all train in its area all the time. Each train computes a braking curve with the MA which is generated based on the location of the train (or some obstacles) in front of it. Therefore, the current train can stop before it reaches the front train in order to keep safe. Theoretically, as long as each train is traveling at the same speed and have the same braking capability, they can travel as close as a few meters between them. However, the unreliable wireless communications can lead to commu- nication interruptions and long latency in the view of performance which is the current key research of the train-ground communications [21]. Due to the characteristics of wireless communications, the security issues should be one more important point, which could result in safety-related accidents. Besides the security vulnerabilities of wireless communications, there are more and more potential vulnerabilities from the application software, the operation systems, the hardware platforms with the wide application of COTS IT technologies. 2.2. The security situation of CBTC systems Compared with the traditional industrial control systems, the characteristics of CBTC systems could be summarized as follows: • The application of wireless communications brings a more efficient CBTC system. However, it is possible for malicious attackers to il- legal access the control network, which could bring unpredictable effects that are hard to be assessed. • The railway networks are deployed at the wide area across the country which means it offers high opportunities for attackers, and the probabilities of security attacks on CBTC systems are high, especially with the application of open wireless communications. • The COTS technology is widely used in CBTC systems, which is not originally designed for railways. As a result, the security issues are not considered. • The transmission protocols are not verified through security as- sessment, which means some potential vulnerabilities could be used by malicious attackers, and it can result in catastrophic accidents. As mentioned above, CBTC systems rely on the wireless commu- nications between trains and the ground control center [22]. Specifi- cally, it shows different features when the CBTC technologies are used respectively in high-speed railways and urban rail transit systems. The high-speed railway systems apply GSM-R as the primary method of train-ground commu- nic

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - 5G network planning in connecting urban areas for trains service using a genetic algorithm
content.abstract_or_snippet: 2025 2949-8678/© 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC BY license (http:// creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/). E.D. Spyrou and V. Kappatos High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 155–162 networks that can scale for both expected and unforeseen scenarios. 5G- R is envisioned as a unified connectivity solution capable of handling growing data traffic and supporting diverse rail-based use cases. In general, 5G requires both advancements in core networks (e.g., network slicing, virtualization) and the development of the 5G New Radio (NR) air interface. This includes improvements in spectral efficiency, support for higher-order modulation, massive MIMO, and the use of millimeter wave frequencies. Additionally, 5G enhances network capacity through spectrum reuse and network densification, which involves increasing the number of cell sites, often small cells, to supplement existing macrocellular networks [5]. The deployment of 5G services requires the installation of new 5G infrastructure, known as next-generation Node-B (gNB), across the target area. Something similar is necessary for 5G-R installations. The process of selecting and configuring the locations where this 5G equipment will be installed is commonly referred to as 5G cellular planning [6]. This is a complex task that involves balancing factors such as costs, coverage area, and Electro-Magnetic Field (EMF) regulations. In high speed train scenarios, the installations should take place taking into consideration the speed and coverage while ensuring QoS. 5G-R which should be a standard at a European level as well, is a dedicated version of the 5G network that addresses the specific requirements of railway operations. It emphasizes ultra-reliable, low-latency commu- nication, which is essential for real-time train control, monitoring, and ensuring safety. Additionally, it supports applications like predictive maintenance, trackside surveillance, and passenger information sys- tems. While 5G-R is primarily optimized for railway use, its feature- s—such as high reliability, low latency, and large data capacity—can also cater to certain User Equipment (UE) needs, including real-time streaming, Internet of Things (IoT) connectivity, and remote control applications. However, these capabilities are generally designed with the operational demands of the railway industry in mind, rather

content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: VC/VOBC, RBC/ZC, CI are the critical parts, which means the CBTC security testbed should adopt the real signalling equipment. However, the train can use an emulation product, which can generate the kinetic model and provide the comprehensive para- meters of the train for the control equipment. 6. Conclusions and future work CBTC is the most popular technology for railway transportations and facing the more and more serious security situation. As one typical industrial control system and the critical infrastructure, the security risks of CBTC systems should be controlled and mitigated. Hence, finding the security risk propagation principles, identifying the treats and vulnerabilities, monitoring the security situation of CBTC systems, responding the risks and performing the proper corresponding strate- gies should be all realized. However, due to the closure and other features, the security protection works acted on the CBTC systems are limited. As there are few valuable researches about the railway security, based on the research results of traditional IT systems and industrial control systems, we summarized the significant security issues of CBTC systems. We began our discussion with an overview of the CBTC se- curity characteristics. In particular, the effects of security risks on the CBTC system are analyzed including the safety and the operation effi- ciency. We then discussed the possible potential attack and summarized the current popular security protection strategies including the securing communication, firewalls and intrusion detection/prevention. Finally, we exploited some challenges of CBTC security research, where the relationship between safety and security, resilience, security manage- ment and testbed were discussed. In summary, the research on CBTC security needs much more work and there is still a long way ahead to enhance the security level of CBTC systems due to some challenging issues. In the article, the state-of-the- art in CBTC security is reviewed from both the engineering and the 15 Z. Yu, H. Wang and F. Chen High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 6–17 research results as there is lack of enough theoretical supports for se- curity issues of railway systems. The challenges proposed in the paper can be beneficial for the future research of CBTC security. References [1] X. Yang, H. Yin, J. Wu, et al., Recognizing the critical stations in urban rail net- works: An analysis method based on the smart-card data, IEEE Intell. Trans. Sys. Mag. 11 (1) (2019) 29–35. [2]

content.title: Modeling of RAILWAY LogicS for Reverse Engineering, Verification and Refactoring
content.abstract_or_snippet: reengineering and design review would be necessary, whose description is not in the scope of this work. The use of UMl for verification purposes is not as formal as could be code-based model checking [6], but it easily allows for governing the complexity of real-world systems; further- more, it constitutes the basis of a series of possible analyses and refinements, as explained in this paper. The approach presented in this paper was applied to a real world case-study: the Radio Block centre (RBc) of ERTMS/ETcS (European Railway Traffic Managements System/ European Train control System) [7]. Presently, the certification process of two RBcs developed by our company included the verification approach described in this paper; these RBc are already operational, controlling one of the newly developed Italian high- speed railway lines, connecting Turin to Novara. (The line, activated for the Winter Olympics of Torino 2006, has been operational since February 2006. It is part of the Turin– Milan line, which is still under development.) To give an idea of its complexity, the control logic of the RBc is constituted by more than 250,000 lines of code. RBc software is mainly written using a legacy language, highly ‘proven in use’ in past projects (above all in interlocking applications; see [6]). Due to the similarity of this process-based (As it will be clear in the following of this paper, a process of the logic language refers to attributes and operations of a control entity and must not be confused with a cPU scheduling unit (though the origin of its name is actually related to the cyclic Real-Time scheduling of the operations).) with object-based languages, UMl-based modeling was quite straightfor- ward and the graphical representation provided, as expected, very useful documentation and analysis facilities. The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 locates the approach in the context of related works; Section 3 provides a general description of the approach, regardless of any specific control system, dividing it into three main steps; Section 4 presents ERTMS/ ETcS and then shows by an example the application of the approach to the control logic of the Radio Block center; Section 5 summarizes results and future developments. 2 RElATED WORkS The work presented in this paper is based on the UMl. UMl is widely accepted as the de facto standard in software engineering; its syntax and semantic have been standardized

content.title: Modeling of RAILWAY LogicS for Reverse Engineering, Verification and Refactoring
content.abstract_or_snippet: F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) 77–94 MODElINg OF RAIlWAY lOgIcS FOR REVERSE ENgINEERINg, VERIFIcATION AND REFAcTORINg F. FlAMMINI1,2, A. lAZZARO1 & N. MAZZOccA2 1ANSAlDO STS, RAMS Unit, Via Nuova delle Brecce 260, Naples 80147, Italy. 2University of Naples “Federico II”, Dipartimento di Informatica e Sistemistica, Via claudio 21, Naples 80125, Italy. ABSTRAcT Model-based approaches are widespread both in functional and non-functional verification activities of critical computer-based systems. Reverse engineering can also be used to support checks for correctness of system implementation against its requirements. In this paper, we show how a model-based technique, using the Unified Modeling language (UMl), suits the reverse engineering of complex control logics. UMl is usually exploited to drive the development of software systems, using an object-oriented and bottom-up approach; however, it can be also used to model legacy non-object-oriented logic processes featuring a clear distinction between data structures and related operations. Our case-study consists in the most important component of the European Railway Traffic Management System/European Train control System: the Radio Block center (RBc). The model we obtained from the logic code of the RBc significantly facilitated both structural and behavioral analyses, giving a valuable contribution to the static verification and refactoring of the software under test. Keywords: control software, modeling, railways, refactoring, reverse engineering, verification. 1 INTRODUcTION AND BAckgROUND A large class of fault tolerant systems is constituted by mission and safety critical apparels employed in industrial control applications, like defense, aerospace and transportation. In fact, most control systems nowadays feature complex computer-based architectures. critical control systems require a number of thorough verification and validation activities, which are regulated by international standards (see for instance [1, 2]). With reference to software veri- fication, such activities can be roughly divided into static and dynamic ones: static analyses do not require code execution, while dynamic ones require execution on the target hardware or in proper simulation environments. Theoretically, static analyses should precede dynamic ones in the system life cycle, with

content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: the track circuit and Balise are used to transmit the control command and the cost is the reduction of running speed from 350 km/h or 300 km/h to 250 km/h. Therefore, the traditional design of CBTC systems includes some safety mechanism which can increase the system resilience. However, the security related design is relatively infrequent, such as the appli- cation of the Railway Signal Safety Protocol (RSSP) which is based on the EN50159 standards [28,29]. However, the existed security protec- tion mechanisms are designed for the single packet or network node. Therefore, the coordinated attack possibly affect the confidentiality or integrity, which has hacked the Ukrainian power grid. In order to eliminate the security threats and develop efficient defense strategies, we need to find the vulnerabilities of CBTC systems based on the exited mechanisms. 2.3. Technical defects In the section, we analyze the characteristics of train ground com- munications and the critical devices, and the corresponding technical defects are summarized. Based on the fail-safe mechanisms, some se- curity strategies are applied which is designed for safety. However, due to being lack of systematic and pertinent security solutions, the CBTC systems are facing rigorous security risks. Train Ground Communication Systems: In CBTC systems, the in- formation transmission process could be treated as a triangle as shown in Fig. 3, where the communication process is periodic. For each communication cycle, every train should report its status including the velocity and the location to the RBC/ZC, and the RBC/ZC will generate a Movement Authority (MA) to every train, which could tell the train where the nearest train or the obstacle (e.g. stations) and the train will compute a recommended speed curve in order to guarantee the train stop before the end of the nearest train or the obstacle. During the transmission process, the train adopts the train-ground wireless com- munications to transmit its status to the base station or receive the MA from the base station. Meanwhile, the transmission between the base station and RBC/ZC is through wire communication. Obviously, the wireless communication is easy to be affected as it works at the open space. As a matter of facts, the operation environment of train-ground communications is complicated, e.g. free space [30,31], leaky wave- guide [32,33] and leaky coaxial cable [34] for urban rail transit sys- tems, free space, leaky coaxial cable for high speed railway. metadata.task_rationale: Gibt Marktüberblick zu verfügbaren RBC-Systemen, Releases und typischen Features als Indikator des Entwicklungsstands.

domain_applicability.reason: Allgemeine Sicherheitsmechanismen/Standards (EN50159, RSSP) in Bahnsteuerung; für RBC-Kontext teilweise nutzbar, jedoch keine herstellerspezifischen RBC-Vergleiche.

hypotheses.statement: In ETCS Level 2 ist ein RBC typischerweise in regionale Zuständigkeitsbereiche unterteilt, verwaltet nur eine begrenzte Anzahl von Streckenabschnitten und erfordert daher Hand-Over-Prozeduren sowie Schnittstellen zu benachbarten RBCs, um durchgehende Zugfahrten zu ermöglichen.
hypotheses.reasoning: Das Dokument beschreibt, dass ein RBC „usually able to manage a limited number of track sections“ und deshalb an „adjacent RBCs“ angebunden werden muss; der Übergang wird als komplexe Hand-Over-Prozedur charakterisiert (ETCS L2 Kontext).

hypotheses.statement: Der Stand der Technik in der RBC-Softwareentwicklung umfasst eine modularisierte Prozess-Architektur mit separaten Application-Prozessen (externe Interaktion, z.B. Interlocking- und Zugkommunikation) und Logic-Prozessen (vitales RBC-Steuerlogikprogramm, u.a. Movement-Authority-Integritätsprüfung), wobei Logik oft in domänenspezifischen/legacy Logiksprachen implementiert und durch einen Logic Manager interpretiert/scheduliert wird.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die Quelle skizziert explizit eine RBC-Softwarearchitektur mit „application processes“ (C safe subset) und „logic processes“ (application-specific logic language) sowie Aufgaben wie MA-Integritätsprüfung und die Rolle eines Logic Managers.

hypotheses.statement: RBC-Technologie ist seit mindestens Mitte der 2000er Jahre in SIL-relevanten Umgebungen operativ im Einsatz; Safety-/Zertifizierungspraktiken nutzen dabei CENELEC-konforme Verifikationsaktivitäten (z.B. EN 50126) und ergänzende modellbasierte Reverse-Engineering-/UML-Methoden zur statischen Verifikation/Refaktorisierung großer RBC-Logiken (Größenordnung >250.000 LOC).
hypotheses.reasoning: Das Fallbeispiel berichtet von zwei bereits operativen RBCs auf der italienischen Hochgeschwindigkeitslinie Turin–Novara (seit 02/2006) und nennt die Komplexität (>250k LOC) sowie die Einbettung in Zertifizierungsprozesse; zudem wird EN 50126 als relevanter Standard für V&V/ RAMS-Nachweis referenziert und der Einsatz UML-basierter Reverse-Engineering-Verifikation beschrieben.

hypotheses.statement: Ein wesentlicher Entwicklungs- und Forschungsfokus für RBC-nahe Systeme liegt aktuell auf der Erhöhung von Performanz und Cybersecurity der Zug-Funkkommunikation: GSM-R weist strukturelle Sicherheitsdefizite (u.a. fehlende Ende-zu-Ende-Verschlüsselung, einseitige Authentisierung) auf, und Kommunikationsunterbrechungen/hohe Latenzen werden als performancekritisch beschrieben; daraus folgt, dass Migrations-/Schutzmaßnahmen (z.B. abgesicherte Protokolle/Netze) für RBC-Betrieb ein zentraler Weiterentwicklungstreiber sind.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die Survey beschreibt GSM-R-Schwächen (keine E2E-Verschlüsselung, one-way authentication) und verweist auf performancekritische Latenz/Unterbrechungen in der drahtlosen Zug-Boden-Kommunikation; RBC/ZC wird als MA-generierende Instanz im Kommunikationszyklus eingeordnet, wodurch diese Funkaspekte für den Entwicklungsstand relevant werden.

content.title: Modeling of RAILWAY LogicS for Reverse Engineering, Verification and Refactoring
content.abstract_or_snippet: Interlocking (IXl) system, which is not standardized in ERTMS. Moreover, the RBc is usually able to manage a limited number of track sections, thus it must be connected to other adjacent RBcs in order to allow for the so-called train Hand-Over (HO). The Hand-Over is the complex procedure that manages the passage of a train from the area supervised by a RBc to the area of adjacent RBcs. In ERTMS level 2, the on-board and track- side communicate by the gSM-R radio network using the Euroradio protocol (see Fig. 3). The software architecture of the RBc is depicted in Fig. 3. It consists of some ‘application’ processes (written in a safe subset of the c programming language), which manage the inter- action of the RBc with the external entities (i.e. other subsystems and the RBc operator), and some ‘logic’ processes, which implement the control logic of the RBc; the latter are written in an application specific logic language, as already mentioned above. The logic Manager has to interpret logic language, translating it into executable code, and to schedule the logic processes. The shaded rectangles in Fig. 4 represent the logic processes, which are the target of our work. For instance, the ‘MA’ process, shown in Fig. 3, verifies the integrity of the Movement Authority assigned to the train against all the significant track and route conditions. Such conditions are received from the interlocking system by means of the ‘IXl interaction’ application process and are managed by the ‘Tc’ logic process, which stores Track circuit physical conditions in its internal variables. If the MA integrity verifica- tion fails (for instance a track circuit is not clear or involved in an emergency condition), the 86 F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) Figure 3: ERTMS/ETcS level 2 reference architecture for the trackside subsystem. Figure 4: The software architecture of the Radio Block center. MA process has to command the sending of an emergency message to the ‘Train interaction’ application process, which will manage the sending of the proper radio message to the train. content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: the architecture of the typical CBTC system is in- troduced, and the characteristics of CBTC systems are summarized which are much different from the other ICS. Therefore, the security issue of CBTC systems are present. 2.1. A typical CBTC system The aim of CBTC is to improve the efficiency of train operation through the application of wireless communications, and the level of system safety should also be enhanced. Fig. 1 describes a typical CBTC system, which consists of on-board subsystems, Data Communication Systems (DCS) and trackside subsystems. The trackside subsystems are connected through the wired (Ethernet) network, which provide the movement. The Movement Authority (MA) for on-board systems through the wireless network. Similarly, the information of trains including the current location and the speed is transmitted to the trackside subsystems which helps to calculate the MA. Although the same system architecture is ap- plied, the high speed railway systems are much more complicated than the urban rail transit systems and some distinctions are shown in Table 1. In the view of functions, the VOBC includes Automatic Train Operation (ATO) and Automatic Train Protection (ATP), but there is no ATO for high speed railways. For trackside subsystems, the RBC and the ZC almost plays the same role, which is to generate and transmit the MA information. One big difference is the main method of train-ground wireless communica- tions, where the high speed railway is using GSM-R and the urban rail transit is using WLAN or other COTS technologies. The railway line is usually divided into areas or regions. Each area is under the control of a ZC or RBC and has its own wireless transmission Fig. 1. content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: [22]. Specifi- cally, it shows different features when the CBTC technologies are used respectively in high-speed railways and urban rail transit systems. The high-speed railway systems apply GSM-R as the primary method of train-ground commu- nications while the WLANs are adopted in urban rail transit systems. According to the analysis on GSM-R [23], there is no end to end encryption in GSM-R protocols, which means the wireless link between Mobile Stations (MS) and Base Stations (BS) is encrypted while the information between BS and BS is transmitted in clear texts. What’s more, the GSM- R uses one-way authentication, which means the network (BS) needs to authenticate the MS when it tries to access but there is no the same authentication process when the BS tries to communication with the MS. As a result, some active attacks can effi- ciently steal information transmitted in the GSM-R network in the form of false BS and make the network collapse. As WLANs work at the public frequency band, the urban rail transit systems face the same security risks compared with high-speed railway systems. Firstly, the interference is unavoidable, which has attracted the attention of subway operators and some research has been done [24–26]. Secondly, as the 802.11 protocols are not originally designed for railways, it shows some problems when WLANs are applied as the main method of train-ground communications, such as the handoff mechanisms [21]. Above all, the working frequency of WLANs is high and the coverage of one AP is small, which is 300500 m. As a result, the handoff happens frequently when the train travels with the high speed, and it provides more opportunities for attackers to access the network with the deficiency of WLAN handoff mechanisms. In fact, there are various WLAN products of different manufactures in urban rail transit systems, including Cisco, Simens, H3C and so on. Because of the im- perfection of 802.11 protocols, there is big difference between different products and it brings much more security issues for CBTC systems. However, as the CBTC systems could also be considered as a typical cyber physical system [27], the CBTC system could be divided into two parts: cyber space and physical space. According to the ANSI/ISA 99 standards, a reference model for ICS systems is proposed, and we build the same one based on the architecture of CBTC systems as shown in Fig. 2. content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: Other COTS Technologies system. Each train transmits its identity (ID), position, direction and ve- locity to the ZC (RBC). The continuous wireless link between each train and the ZC (RBC) can ensure that the ZC (RBC) knows the status in- formation of all train in its area all the time. Each train computes a braking curve with the MA which is generated based on the location of the train (or some obstacles) in front of it. Therefore, the current train can stop before it reaches the front train in order to keep safe. Theoretically, as long as each train is traveling at the same speed and have the same braking capability, they can travel as close as a few meters between them. However, the unreliable wireless communications can lead to commu- nication interruptions and long latency in the view of performance which is the current key research of the train-ground communications [21]. Due to the characteristics of wireless communications, the security issues should be one more important point, which could result in safety-related accidents. Besides the security vulnerabilities of wireless communications, there are more and more potential vulnerabilities from the application software, the operation systems, the hardware platforms with the wide application of COTS IT technologies. 2.2. The security situation of CBTC systems Compared with the traditional industrial control systems, the characteristics of CBTC systems could be summarized as follows: • The application of wireless communications brings a more efficient CBTC system. However, it is possible for malicious attackers to il- legal access the control network, which could bring unpredictable effects that are hard to be assessed. • The railway networks are deployed at the wide area across the country which means it offers high opportunities for attackers, and the probabilities of security attacks on CBTC systems are high, especially with the application of open wireless communications. • The COTS technology is widely used in CBTC systems, which is not originally designed for railways. As a result, the security issues are not considered. • The transmission protocols are not verified through security as- sessment, which means some potential vulnerabilities could be used by malicious attackers, and it can result in catastrophic accidents. As mentioned above, CBTC systems rely on the wireless commu- nications between trains and the ground control center [22]. Specifi- cally, it shows different features when the CBTC technologies are used respectively in high-speed railways and urban rail transit systems. The high-speed railway systems apply GSM-R as the primary method of train-ground commu- nic

content.abstract_or_snippet: 2025 2949-8678/© 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltd. Kappatos High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 155–162 networks that can scale for both expected and unforeseen scenarios. 5G- R is envisioned as a unified connectivity solution capable of handling growing data traffic and supporting diverse rail-based use cases. In general, 5G requires both advancements in core networks (e.g., network slicing, virtualization) and the development of the 5G New Radio (NR) air interface. This includes improvements in spectral efficiency, support for higher-order modulation, massive MIMO, and the use of millimeter wave frequencies. Additionally, 5G enhances network capacity through spectrum reuse and network densification, which involves increasing the number of cell sites, often small cells, to supplement existing macrocellular networks [5]. The deployment of 5G services requires the installation of new 5G infrastructure, known as next-generation Node-B (gNB), across the target area. Something similar is necessary for 5G-R installations. The process of selecting and configuring the locations where this 5G equipment will be installed is commonly referred to as 5G cellular planning [6]. This is a complex task that involves balancing factors such as costs, coverage area, and Electro-Magnetic Field (EMF) regulations. In high speed train scenarios, the installations should take place taking into consideration the speed and coverage while ensuring QoS. 5G-R which should be a standard at a European level as well, is a dedicated version of the 5G network that addresses the specific requirements of railway operations. It emphasizes ultra-reliable, low-latency commu- nication, which is essential for real-time train control, monitoring, and ensuring safety. Additionally, it supports applications like predictive maintenance, trackside surveillance, and passenger information sys- tems. While 5G-R is primarily optimized for railway use, its feature- s—such as high reliability, low latency, and large data capacity—can also cater to certain User Equipment (UE) needs, including real-time streaming, Internet of Things (IoT) connectivity, and remote control applications. However, these capabilities are generally designed with the operational demands of the railway industry in mind, rather

content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: VC/VOBC, RBC/ZC, CI are the critical parts, which means the CBTC security testbed should adopt the real signalling equipment. However, the train can use an emulation product, which can generate the kinetic model and provide the comprehensive para- meters of the train for the control equipment. 6. Conclusions and future work CBTC is the most popular technology for railway transportations and facing the more and more serious security situation. As one typical industrial control system and the critical infrastructure, the security risks of CBTC systems should be controlled and mitigated. Hence, finding the security risk propagation principles, identifying the treats and vulnerabilities, monitoring the security situation of CBTC systems, responding the risks and performing the proper corresponding strate- gies should be all realized. However, due to the closure and other features, the security protection works acted on the CBTC systems are limited. As there are few valuable researches about the railway security, based on the research results of traditional IT systems and industrial control systems, we summarized the significant security issues of CBTC systems. We began our discussion with an overview of the CBTC se- curity characteristics. In particular, the effects of security risks on the CBTC system are analyzed including the safety and the operation effi- ciency. We then discussed the possible potential attack and summarized the current popular security protection strategies including the securing communication, firewalls and intrusion detection/prevention. Finally, we exploited some challenges of CBTC security research, where the relationship between safety and security, resilience, security manage- ment and testbed were discussed. In summary, the research on CBTC security needs much more work and there is still a long way ahead to enhance the security level of CBTC systems due to some challenging issues. In the article, the state-of-the- art in CBTC security is reviewed from both the engineering and the 15 Z. Yu, H. Wang and F. Chen High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 6–17 research results as there is lack of enough theoretical supports for se- curity issues of railway systems. The challenges proposed in the paper can be beneficial for the future research of CBTC security. References [1] X. Yang, H. Yin, J. Wu, et al., Recognizing the critical stations in urban rail net- works: An analysis method based on the smart-card data, IEEE Intell. Trans. Sys. Mag. 11 (1) (2019) 29–35. [2]

content.title: Modeling of RAILWAY LogicS for Reverse Engineering, Verification and Refactoring
content.abstract_or_snippet: reengineering and design review would be necessary, whose description is not in the scope of this work. The use of UMl for verification purposes is not as formal as could be code-based model checking [6], but it easily allows for governing the complexity of real-world systems; further- more, it constitutes the basis of a series of possible analyses and refinements, as explained in this paper. The approach presented in this paper was applied to a real world case-study: the Radio Block centre (RBc) of ERTMS/ETcS (European Railway Traffic Managements System/ European Train control System) [7]. Presently, the certification process of two RBcs developed by our company included the verification approach described in this paper; these RBc are already operational, controlling one of the newly developed Italian high- speed railway lines, connecting Turin to Novara. (The line, activated for the Winter Olympics of Torino 2006, has been operational since February 2006. It is part of the Turin– Milan line, which is still under development.) To give an idea of its complexity, the control logic of the RBc is constituted by more than 250,000 lines of code. RBc software is mainly written using a legacy language, highly ‘proven in use’ in past projects (above all in interlocking applications; see [6]). Due to the similarity of this process-based (As it will be clear in the following of this paper, a process of the logic language refers to attributes and operations of a control entity and must not be confused with a cPU scheduling unit (though the origin of its name is actually related to the cyclic Real-Time scheduling of the operations).) with object-based languages, UMl-based modeling was quite straightfor- ward and the graphical representation provided, as expected, very useful documentation and analysis facilities. The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 locates the approach in the context of related works; Section 3 provides a general description of the approach, regardless of any specific control system, dividing it into three main steps; Section 4 presents ERTMS/ ETcS and then shows by an example the application of the approach to the control logic of the Radio Block center; Section 5 summarizes results and future developments. 2 RElATED WORkS The work presented in this paper is based on the UMl. UMl is widely accepted as the de facto standard in software engineering; its syntax and semantic have been standardized

content.title: Modeling of RAILWAY LogicS for Reverse Engineering, Verification and Refactoring
content.abstract_or_snippet: F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) 77–94 MODElINg OF RAIlWAY lOgIcS FOR REVERSE ENgINEERINg, VERIFIcATION AND REFAcTORINg F. FlAMMINI1,2, A. lAZZARO1 & N. MAZZOccA2 1ANSAlDO STS, RAMS Unit, Via Nuova delle Brecce 260, Naples 80147, Italy. 2University of Naples “Federico II”, Dipartimento di Informatica e Sistemistica, Via claudio 21, Naples 80125, Italy. ABSTRAcT Model-based approaches are widespread both in functional and non-functional verification activities of critical computer-based systems. Reverse engineering can also be used to support checks for correctness of system implementation against its requirements. In this paper, we show how a model-based technique, using the Unified Modeling language (UMl), suits the reverse engineering of complex control logics. UMl is usually exploited to drive the development of software systems, using an object-oriented and bottom-up approach; however, it can be also used to model legacy non-object-oriented logic processes featuring a clear distinction between data structures and related operations. Our case-study consists in the most important component of the European Railway Traffic Management System/European Train control System: the Radio Block center (RBc). The model we obtained from the logic code of the RBc significantly facilitated both structural and behavioral analyses, giving a valuable contribution to the static verification and refactoring of the software under test. Keywords: control software, modeling, railways, refactoring, reverse engineering, verification. 1 INTRODUcTION AND BAckgROUND A large class of fault tolerant systems is constituted by mission and safety critical apparels employed in industrial control applications, like defense, aerospace and transportation. In fact, most control systems nowadays feature complex computer-based architectures. critical control systems require a number of thorough verification and validation activities, which are regulated by international standards (see for instance [1, 2]). With reference to software veri- fication, such activities can be roughly divided into static and dynamic ones: static analyses do not require code execution, while dynamic ones require execution on the target hardware or in proper simulation environments. Theoretically, static analyses should precede dynamic ones in the system life cycle, with

content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: the track circuit and Balise are used to transmit the control command and the cost is the reduction of running speed from 350 km/h or 300 km/h to 250 km/h. Therefore, the traditional design of CBTC systems includes some safety mechanism which can increase the system resilience. However, the security related design is relatively infrequent, such as the appli- cation of the Railway Signal Safety Protocol (RSSP) which is based on the EN50159 standards [28,29]. However, the existed security protec- tion mechanisms are designed for the single packet or network node. Therefore, the coordinated attack possibly affect the confidentiality or integrity, which has hacked the Ukrainian power grid. In order to eliminate the security threats and develop efficient defense strategies, we need to find the vulnerabilities of CBTC systems based on the exited mechanisms. 2.3. Technical defects In the section, we analyze the characteristics of train ground com- munications and the critical devices, and the corresponding technical defects are summarized. Based on the fail-safe mechanisms, some se- curity strategies are applied which is designed for safety. However, due to being lack of systematic and pertinent security solutions, the CBTC systems are facing rigorous security risks. Train Ground Communication Systems: In CBTC systems, the in- formation transmission process could be treated as a triangle as shown in Fig. 3, where the communication process is periodic. For each communication cycle, every train should report its status including the velocity and the location to the RBC/ZC, and the RBC/ZC will generate a Movement Authority (MA) to every train, which could tell the train where the nearest train or the obstacle (e.g. stations) and the train will compute a recommended speed curve in order to guarantee the train stop before the end of the nearest train or the obstacle. During the transmission process, the train adopts the train-ground wireless com- munications to transmit its status to the base station or receive the MA from the base station. Meanwhile, the transmission between the base station and RBC/ZC is through wire communication. Obviously, the wireless communication is easy to be affected as it works at the open space. As a matter of facts, the operation environment of train-ground communications is complicated, e.g. free space [30,31], leaky wave- guide [32,33] and leaky coaxial cable [34] for urban rail transit sys- tems, free space, leaky coaxial cable for high speed railway. updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Teilweise gestützt: Survey beschreibt GSM-R-Sicherheitsdefizite (keine E2E-Verschlüsselung, einseitige Authentisierung) sowie Performanceprobleme (Unterbrechungen/Latenz) als aktuelles Forschungsfeld der Zug-Boden-Kommunikation; RBC/ZC wird als MA-generierende Instanz im Kommunikationszyklus genannt. Allerdings bezieht sich die Quelle auf CBTC/High-speed Railway Security allgemein; der Schluss ‚wesentlicher Entwicklungsfokus für RBC-nahe Systeme‘ ist plausibel, aber nicht direkt als RBC-spezifischer Entwicklungsstand belegt.

content.title: Modeling of RAILWAY LogicS for Reverse Engineering, Verification and Refactoring
content.abstract_or_snippet: reengineering and design review would be necessary, whose description is not in the scope of this work. The use of UMl for verification purposes is not as formal as could be code-based model checking [6], but it easily allows for governing the complexity of real-world systems; further- more, it constitutes the basis of a series of possible analyses and refinements, as explained in this paper. The approach presented in this paper was applied to a real world case-study: the Radio Block centre (RBc) of ERTMS/ETcS (European Railway Traffic Managements System/ European Train control System) [7]. Presently, the certification process of two RBcs developed by our company included the verification approach described in this paper; these RBc are already operational, controlling one of the newly developed Italian high- speed railway lines, connecting Turin to Novara. (The line, activated for the Winter Olympics of Torino 2006, has been operational since February 2006. It is part of the Turin– Milan line, which is still under development.) To give an idea of its complexity, the control logic of the RBc is constituted by more than 250,000 lines of code. RBc software is mainly written using a legacy language, highly ‘proven in use’ in past projects (above all in interlocking applications; see [6]). Due to the similarity of this process-based (As it will be clear in the following of this paper, a process of the logic language refers to attributes and operations of a control entity and must not be confused with a cPU scheduling unit (though the origin of its name is actually related to the cyclic Real-Time scheduling of the operations).) with object-based languages, UMl-based modeling was quite straightfor- ward and the graphical representation provided, as expected, very useful documentation and analysis facilities. The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 locates the approach in the context of related works; Section 3 provides a general description of the approach, regardless of any specific control system, dividing it into three main steps; Section 4 presents ERTMS/ ETcS and then shows by an example the application of the approach to the control logic of the Radio Block center; Section 5 summarizes results and future developments. 2 RElATED WORkS The work presented in this paper is based on the UMl. UMl is widely accepted as the de facto standard in software engineering; its syntax and semantic have been standardized

content.abstract_or_snippet: Interlocking (IXl) system, which is not standardized in ERTMS. Moreover, the RBc is usually able to manage a limited number of track sections, thus it must be connected to other adjacent RBcs in order to allow for the so-called train Hand-Over (HO). The Hand-Over is the complex procedure that manages the passage of a train from the area supervised by a RBc to the area of adjacent RBcs. In ERTMS level 2, the on-board and track- side communicate by the gSM-R radio network using the Euroradio protocol (see Fig. 3). The software architecture of the RBc is depicted in Fig. 3. It consists of some ‘application’ processes (written in a safe subset of the c programming language), which manage the inter- action of the RBc with the external entities (i.e. other subsystems and the RBc operator), and some ‘logic’ processes, which implement the control logic of the RBc; the latter are written in an application specific logic language, as already mentioned above. The logic Manager has to interpret logic language, translating it into executable code, and to schedule the logic processes. The shaded rectangles in Fig. 4 represent the logic processes, which are the target of our work. For instance, the ‘MA’ process, shown in Fig. 3, verifies the integrity of the Movement Authority assigned to the train against all the significant track and route conditions. Such conditions are received from the interlocking system by means of the ‘IXl interaction’ application process and are managed by the ‘Tc’ logic process, which stores Track circuit physical conditions in its internal variables. If the MA integrity verifica- tion fails (for instance a track circuit is not clear or involved in an emergency condition), the 86 F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) Figure 3: ERTMS/ETcS level 2 reference architecture for the trackside subsystem. Figure 4: The software architecture of the Radio Block center. MA process has to command the sending of an emergency message to the ‘Train interaction’ application process, which will manage the sending of the proper radio message to the train. content.abstract_or_snippet: in the language of dynamic binding, pointers or references; in fact, the language was specifically developed to be understandable by non-programmers. The result is that the reverse engi- neering procedure can easily be automated, in analogy with computer Aided Software Engineering (cASE) tools featuring ‘round-trip’ engineering facilities suitable for general purpose languages (see e.g. [15]). Differently from such tools, the representation provided by the proprietary tool we are presently developing is application specific, that is to say it provides a representation fitting the specific needs of test engineers (a general and difficult to check representation would not prove useful for verification purposes). Next section will provide an example application of the model-based reverse engineering approach to the RBc case-study. 4.2 Reverse engineering, static verification and refactoring of the RBc In this section, the modeling technique from RBc logic language to UMl diagrams is described by referring to the simple ‘change of Traction Power’ (cTP) logic process. To give an idea of its relative complexity, such process is nearly 20 times smaller in terms of lines of code with respect to the MA process (the latter being very difficult to describe in detail with- out an in depth knowledge of the RBc software architecture). The cTP process has to manage manual commands of activation/deactivation of a change of traction power line section coming from the RBc operator. The activation/deactivation commands must be accepted only if the track circuit in which there is a change of power is free and not included in a MA assigned to a train. In the following we present the UMl based modeling and analysis for such example process. 4.2.1 Use-case diagrams Use-case diagrams represent how a logic process can be used by application processes or by the operator using manual commands; therefore, they represent the externally triggered 88 F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) high-level procedures (see Fig. 5). Usually, at least one use-case diagram is specified for each system level scenario (e.g. Start of Mission, Hand-Over, etc.). Use-case diagrams are significant in representing all the possible functional scenarios involving the logic process, hence giving the possibility to verifiers to check the validity of the actions performed on the process at a very high abstraction level; however, they reveal not so useful for refactoring purposes. 4.2.2 class diagrams class diagrams

content.title: Modeling of RAILWAY LogicS for Reverse Engineering, Verification and Refactoring
content.abstract_or_snippet: F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) 77–94 MODElINg OF RAIlWAY lOgIcS FOR REVERSE ENgINEERINg, VERIFIcATION AND REFAcTORINg F. FlAMMINI1,2, A. lAZZARO1 & N. MAZZOccA2 1ANSAlDO STS, RAMS Unit, Via Nuova delle Brecce 260, Naples 80147, Italy. 2University of Naples “Federico II”, Dipartimento di Informatica e Sistemistica, Via claudio 21, Naples 80125, Italy. ABSTRAcT Model-based approaches are widespread both in functional and non-functional verification activities of critical computer-based systems. Reverse engineering can also be used to support checks for correctness of system implementation against its requirements. In this paper, we show how a model-based technique, using the Unified Modeling language (UMl), suits the reverse engineering of complex control logics. UMl is usually exploited to drive the development of software systems, using an object-oriented and bottom-up approach; however, it can be also used to model legacy non-object-oriented logic processes featuring a clear distinction between data structures and related operations. Our case-study consists in the most important component of the European Railway Traffic Management System/European Train control System: the Radio Block center (RBc). The model we obtained from the logic code of the RBc significantly facilitated both structural and behavioral analyses, giving a valuable contribution to the static verification and refactoring of the software under test. Keywords: control software, modeling, railways, refactoring, reverse engineering, verification. 1 INTRODUcTION AND BAckgROUND A large class of fault tolerant systems is constituted by mission and safety critical apparels employed in industrial control applications, like defense, aerospace and transportation. In fact, most control systems nowadays feature complex computer-based architectures. critical control systems require a number of thorough verification and validation activities, which are regulated by international standards (see for instance [1, 2]). With reference to software veri- fication, such activities can be roughly divided into static and dynamic ones: static analyses do not require code execution, while dynamic ones require execution on the target hardware or in proper simulation environments. Theoretically, static analyses should precede dynamic ones in the system life cycle, with

content.abstract_or_snippet: for the traceability analysis. Each ‘composed’ or ‘high- level’ sequence diagram represents a scenario of, e.g. Start of Mission, Hand-Over, Emergency, and so on. Figure 7: Sequence diagram for the cTP logic process. 90 F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) By checking sequence diagrams, we discovered that in some cases the controls on the feasibility of a manual command were missing. This was easy to visually check on the diagrams as well as to be automated by defining a rule according to which such control must be performed for each command received by a process. In particular, the check allowed to detect a missing control in the ES process causing the RBc to safely shut- down when the user tried to send an emergency message to a non-existing train number. Usually, such errors are revealed in the functional testing phase, while the approach described in this paper allowed test engineers to detect them earlier in system V&V proc- ess. Furthermore, by tracing sequence diagrams into functional requirements, we discovered some pieces of code related to never referenced operations, which were inher- ited from an early specification: an application of refactoring, in this case, consisted in removing such pleonastic operations and attributes. Sequence diagrams also prove very useful for refactoring, of which we present in the following a quite general example. From the analysis of sequence diagrams, we realized that most of the RBc logic code was made up by condition verification statements. Such statements are clearly explicated in sequence diagrams, which also show their expected weight in terms of process interactions and thus of elaboration cycles (given the fixed policy, each interaction with an already scheduled process requires a further elaboration cycle). For instance, ‘lighter’ condition verifications only need an access to one or more internal attributes; ‘heavier’ ones involve the interac- tion of two or more processes, and can last several elaboration cycles in the fixed process scheduling scheme. Therefore, by properly grouping and shifting conditions we were able to predict and minimize the average number of controls performed by the RBc, with a significant improvement in system performance, which directly impacted on the number of trains the RBc was able to manage (final gain was about the 30%). For instance, if a Tc is involved in an emergency condition, there is no need to check its occupation status: as stated by the functional requirements, immediately after the emergency

While building the model we exploited all the syntactic controls and automations of the Rational Rose Modeler application, which constituted a first congruity check on the model under construction. Some examples: as the model grows up, the already defined links are automatically added by the application; in sequence diagrams, it is not possible to call an operation which has not been already defined in class diagrams; the accessibility of attributes and operations (public, protected, private) is automatically checked; etc. Next section will provide a general introduction to ERTMS/ETcS. 4.1 ERTMS/ETcS level 2 system implementation ERTMS/ETcS is the specification of a standard aiming at improving safety, performance and interoperability of European railways [7]. In Italy, the so-called level 2 specification of ERTMS/ETcS is implemented in high-speed railway lines. ERTMS/ETcS specifies three main subsystems: trackside, on-board and lineside. The trackside subsystem is the ground part of the overall system, and manages railway signaling to ensure safe train separation and rout- ing. The most important trackside subsystem is the Radio Block center, which has the aim of collecting track and train information in order to provide trains with necessary data: Move- ment Authorities (MA), that is the distance trains are authorized to move on; Static Speed Profiles (SSP), that is maximum speed limits allowed by the track; possible Emergency Stops (ES). The on-board control system performs train protection by controlling train speed against the elaborated dynamic speed profiles (or braking curves). The lineside is constituted by the so-called balises, which are devices positioned between the track lines, which have the aim to transmit geographical positioning data to the trains passing over them; such data is used by the on-board system in order to build and send position reports to the trackside. The Radio Block center is responsible of providing train headways by using data received in train position reports and delivering the correct MA, SSP and ES messages to the trains. In order to detect the necessary route and Track circuit (Tc) occupation status, RBc is con- nected to the national Interlocking (IXl) system, which is not standardized in ERTMS. Moreover, the RBc is usually able to manage a limited number of track sections, thus it must be connected to other adjacent RBcs in order to allow for the

A typical CBTC system The aim of CBTC is to improve the efficiency of train operation through the application of wireless communications, and the level of system safety should also be enhanced. Fig. 1 describes a typical CBTC system, which consists of on-board subsystems, Data Communication Systems (DCS) and trackside subsystems. The trackside subsystems are connected through the wired (Ethernet) network, which provide the movement. The Movement Authority (MA) for on-board systems through the wireless network. Similarly, the information of trains including the current location and the speed is transmitted to the trackside subsystems which helps to calculate the MA. Although the same system architecture is ap- plied, the high speed railway systems are much more complicated than the urban rail transit systems and some distinctions are shown in Table 1. In the view of functions, the VOBC includes Automatic Train Operation (ATO) and Automatic Train Protection (ATP), but there is no ATO for high speed railways. For trackside subsystems, the RBC and the ZC almost plays the same role, which is to generate and transmit the MA information. One big difference is the main method of train-ground wireless communica- tions, where the high speed railway is using GSM-R and the urban rail transit is using WLAN or other COTS technologies. The railway line is usually divided into areas or regions. Each area is under the control of a ZC or RBC and has its own wireless transmission Fig. 1. content.abstract_or_snippet: illegal or incompatible with process status, in order to tolerate casual (e.g. soft-errors in memories) as well as possibly systematic faults. Moreover, refactoring can involve the grouping of condition checks, the re-ordering of checks (weighted by occurrence probability and/or by the number of necessary steps to perform the check of the condition) and other specific performance optimizations. The use of sequence diagrams allows test engineers to precisely weigh the condition checks in terms of needed interactions between logic processes, and thus in terms of elaboration cycles. As for the respect of object-orientation, whenever necessary, operations must be added in class diagrams in order to let processes modify variables of other processes only by using specific methods and not by directly accessing external attributes. Such a modification also allows moving defensive programming controls from the calling methods to the newly added ones, possibly gaining in reliability, readability and code length. The behavioral impact of such modifications can be easily checked by means of UMl sequence and state diagrams. In particular, state diagrams also allow for behavioral refine- ments, e.g. aimed at moving/grouping states in composite ones. As compliance with high-level requirements has already been verified in the previous step, it is necessary that such transfor- mations preserve the logic behavior of the system. 4 AN INDUSTRIAl APPlIcATION: THE RADIO BlOck cENTER In our experience with the RBc, almost all the activities described in Section 3 were per- formed by hand; in fact, while the building and verification of the model is theoretically automatable, when the verification process started we were not completely confident about the worth of automating it (see Section 5 for future developments). As aforementioned, the structure of RBc logic processes well suits to be modeled using an object-based language: logic processes can be easily thought of as classes, with their internal ‘variables’ constituting the attributes, and their ‘commands’ being the operations. In particular, the ‘process status’ variable has been represented in a specific state diagram, as it triggers most of the process F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) 85 behavior and thus is very critical. The modeling tool we used to build UMl diagrams was the Rational Rose Modeler Edition [9]. Moreover, the RBc is usually able to manage a limited number of track sections, thus it must be connected to other adjacent RBcs in order to allow for the so-called train Hand-Over (HO). The Hand-Over is the complex procedure that manages the passage of a train from the area supervised by a RBc to the area of adjacent RBcs. In ERTMS level 2, the on-board and track- side communicate by the gSM-R radio network using the Euroradio protocol (see Fig. 3). The software architecture of the RBc is depicted in Fig. 3. It consists of some ‘application’ processes (written in a safe subset of the c programming language), which manage the inter- action of the RBc with the external entities (i.e. other subsystems and the RBc operator), and some ‘logic’ processes, which implement the control logic of the RBc; the latter are written in an application specific logic language, as already mentioned above. The logic Manager has to interpret logic language, translating it into executable code, and to schedule the logic processes. The shaded rectangles in Fig. 4 represent the logic processes, which are the target of our work. For instance, the ‘MA’ process, shown in Fig. 3, verifies the integrity of the Movement Authority assigned to the train against all the significant track and route conditions. Such conditions are received from the interlocking system by means of the ‘IXl interaction’ application process and are managed by the ‘Tc’ logic process, which stores Track circuit physical conditions in its internal variables. If the MA integrity verifica- tion fails (for instance a track circuit is not clear or involved in an emergency condition), the 86 F. Flammini, et al., Int. MA process has to command the sending of an emergency message to the ‘Train interaction’ application process, which will manage the sending of the proper radio message to the train. content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: Other COTS Technologies system. Each train transmits its identity (ID), position, direction and ve- locity to the ZC (RBC). The continuous wireless link between each train and the ZC (RBC) can ensure that the ZC (RBC) knows the status in- formation of all train in its area all the time. Each train computes a braking curve with the MA which is generated based on the location of the train (or some obstacles) in front of it. Therefore, the current train can stop before it reaches the front train in order to keep safe. Theoretically, as long as each train is traveling at the same speed and have the same braking capability, they can travel as close as a few meters between them. However, the unreliable wireless communications can lead to commu- nication interruptions and long latency in the view of performance which is the current key research of the train-ground communications [21]. Due to the characteristics of wireless communications, the security issues should be one more important point, which could result in safety-related accidents. Besides the security vulnerabilities of wireless communications, there are more and more potential vulnerabilities from the application software, the operation systems, the hardware platforms with the wide application of COTS IT technologies. 2.2. The security situation of CBTC systems Compared with the traditional industrial control systems, the characteristics of CBTC systems could be summarized as follows: • The application of wireless communications brings a more efficient CBTC system. However, it is possible for malicious attackers to il- legal access the control network, which could bring unpredictable effects that are hard to be assessed. • The railway networks are deployed at the wide area across the country which means it offers high opportunities for attackers, and the probabilities of security attacks on CBTC systems are high, especially with the application of open wireless communications. • The COTS technology is widely used in CBTC systems, which is not originally designed for railways. As a result, the security issues are not considered. • The transmission protocols are not verified through security as- sessment, which means some potential vulnerabilities could be used by malicious attackers, and it can result in catastrophic accidents. As mentioned above, CBTC systems rely on the wireless commu- nications between trains and the ground control center [22]. Specifi- cally, it shows different features when the CBTC technologies are used respectively in high-speed railways and urban rail transit systems. The high-speed railway systems apply GSM-R as the primary method of train-ground commu- nic

content.abstract_or_snippet: the track circuit and Balise are used to transmit the control command and the cost is the reduction of running speed from 350 km/h or 300 km/h to 250 km/h. Therefore, the traditional design of CBTC systems includes some safety mechanism which can increase the system resilience. However, the security related design is relatively infrequent, such as the appli- cation of the Railway Signal Safety Protocol (RSSP) which is based on the EN50159 standards [28,29]. However, the existed security protec- tion mechanisms are designed for the single packet or network node. Therefore, the coordinated attack possibly affect the confidentiality or integrity, which has hacked the Ukrainian power grid. In order to eliminate the security threats and develop efficient defense strategies, we need to find the vulnerabilities of CBTC systems based on the exited mechanisms. 2.3. Technical defects In the section, we analyze the characteristics of train ground com- munications and the critical devices, and the corresponding technical defects are summarized. Based on the fail-safe mechanisms, some se- curity strategies are applied which is designed for safety. However, due to being lack of systematic and pertinent security solutions, the CBTC systems are facing rigorous security risks. Train Ground Communication Systems: In CBTC systems, the in- formation transmission process could be treated as a triangle as shown in Fig. 3, where the communication process is periodic. For each communication cycle, every train should report its status including the velocity and the location to the RBC/ZC, and the RBC/ZC will generate a Movement Authority (MA) to every train, which could tell the train where the nearest train or the obstacle (e.g. stations) and the train will compute a recommended speed curve in order to guarantee the train stop before the end of the nearest train or the obstacle. During the transmission process, the train adopts the train-ground wireless com- munications to transmit its status to the base station or receive the MA from the base station. Meanwhile, the transmission between the base station and RBC/ZC is through wire communication. Obviously, the wireless communication is easy to be affected as it works at the open space. As a matter of facts, the operation environment of train-ground communications is complicated, e.g. free space [30,31], leaky wave- guide [32,33] and leaky coaxial cable [34] for urban rail transit sys- tems, free space, leaky coaxial cable for high speed railway.
