content.title: Modeling of RAILWAY LogicS for Reverse Engineering, Verification and Refactoring
content.abstract_or_snippet: used to build UMl diagrams was the Rational Rose Modeler Edition [9]. While building the model we exploited all the syntactic controls and automations of the Rational Rose Modeler application, which constituted a first congruity check on the model under construction. Some examples: as the model grows up, the already defined links are automatically added by the application; in sequence diagrams, it is not possible to call an operation which has not been already defined in class diagrams; the accessibility of attributes and operations (public, protected, private) is automatically checked; etc. Next section will provide a general introduction to ERTMS/ETcS. 4.1 ERTMS/ETcS level 2 system implementation ERTMS/ETcS is the specification of a standard aiming at improving safety, performance and interoperability of European railways [7]. In Italy, the so-called level 2 specification of ERTMS/ETcS is implemented in high-speed railway lines. ERTMS/ETcS specifies three main subsystems: trackside, on-board and lineside. The trackside subsystem is the ground part of the overall system, and manages railway signaling to ensure safe train separation and rout- ing. The most important trackside subsystem is the Radio Block center, which has the aim of collecting track and train information in order to provide trains with necessary data: Move- ment Authorities (MA), that is the distance trains are authorized to move on; Static Speed Profiles (SSP), that is maximum speed limits allowed by the track; possible Emergency Stops (ES). The on-board control system performs train protection by controlling train speed against the elaborated dynamic speed profiles (or braking curves). The lineside is constituted by the so-called balises, which are devices positioned between the track lines, which have the aim to transmit geographical positioning data to the trains passing over them; such data is used by the on-board system in order to build and send position reports to the trackside. The Radio Block center is responsible of providing train headways by using data received in train position reports and delivering the correct MA, SSP and ES messages to the trains. In order to detect the necessary route and Track circuit (Tc) occupation status, RBc is con- nected to the national Interlocking (IXl) system, which is not standardized in ERTMS. Moreover, the RBc is usually able to manage a limited number of track sections, thus it must be connected to other adjacent RBcs in order to allow for the

Figure 7: Sequence diagram for the cTP logic process. 90 F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) By checking sequence diagrams, we discovered that in some cases the controls on the feasibility of a manual command were missing. This was easy to visually check on the diagrams as well as to be automated by defining a rule according to which such control must be performed for each command received by a process. In particular, the check allowed to detect a missing control in the ES process causing the RBc to safely shut- down when the user tried to send an emergency message to a non-existing train number. Usually, such errors are revealed in the functional testing phase, while the approach described in this paper allowed test engineers to detect them earlier in system V&V proc- ess. Furthermore, by tracing sequence diagrams into functional requirements, we discovered some pieces of code related to never referenced operations, which were inher- ited from an early specification: an application of refactoring, in this case, consisted in removing such pleonastic operations and attributes. Sequence diagrams also prove very useful for refactoring, of which we present in the following a quite general example. From the analysis of sequence diagrams, we realized that most of the RBc logic code was made up by condition verification statements. Such statements are clearly explicated in sequence diagrams, which also show their expected weight in terms of process interactions and thus of elaboration cycles (given the fixed policy, each interaction with an already scheduled process requires a further elaboration cycle). For instance, ‘lighter’ condition verifications only need an access to one or more internal attributes; ‘heavier’ ones involve the interac- tion of two or more processes, and can last several elaboration cycles in the fixed process scheduling scheme. Therefore, by properly grouping and shifting conditions we were able to predict and minimize the average number of controls performed by the RBc, with a significant improvement in system performance, which directly impacted on the number of trains the RBc was able to manage (final gain was about the 30%). For instance, if a Tc is involved in an emergency condition, there is no need to check its occupation status: as stated by the functional requirements, immediately after the emergency

content.abstract_or_snippet: RBc was able to manage (final gain was about the 30%). For instance, if a Tc is involved in an emergency condition, there is no need to check its occupation status: as stated by the functional requirements, immediately after the emergency is detected, an emergency message must be sent to the train. Thus, it would be natural to set such control as the first to be performed. However, the probability of an emergency is very low, and such a check would be false most of the times, wasting elaboration time. Therefore, start- ing from the consideration that an emergency must always include all the Tc of a track section (at least 3), a more effective design review consisted in adding a new process named Track Section and making the MA process perform a single check on this process instead of performing it for each Tc. Please note that real-time constraint are not taken into account in the specific application (ERTMS/ETcS trackside subsystem) as the on-board system is responsible for reacting to excessive delays in receiving messages from the RBc. The reaction consists in the applica- tion of service or emergency brakes activated by a proper channel vitality monitor, as specified by the standard, which also protects from loss of radio packets due to gSM-R unavailability. Therefore, the delay in sending messages by the trackside subsystem only has an impact on system performance (which is tested apart) but not on its safety. 4.2.4 State diagrams State diagrams show the transitions of the state variables of the logic processes against the possible triggering events. The cTP state diagram shown in Fig. 8 has been simplified to be self-explaining. By checking the diagram it is immediately clear that for the cTP process all input conditions at any state have been considered, and there are no unreachable or deadlock states (which have been detected and corrected for some other processes). In the design phase, the state machine of each process of the Radio Block center has been kept enough F. Flammini, et al., Int. content.abstract_or_snippet: the architecture of the typical CBTC system is in- troduced, and the characteristics of CBTC systems are summarized which are much different from the other ICS. Therefore, the security issue of CBTC systems are present. 2.1. A typical CBTC system The aim of CBTC is to improve the efficiency of train operation through the application of wireless communications, and the level of system safety should also be enhanced. Fig. 1 describes a typical CBTC system, which consists of on-board subsystems, Data Communication Systems (DCS) and trackside subsystems. The trackside subsystems are connected through the wired (Ethernet) network, which provide the movement. The Movement Authority (MA) for on-board systems through the wireless network. Similarly, the information of trains including the current location and the speed is transmitted to the trackside subsystems which helps to calculate the MA. Although the same system architecture is ap- plied, the high speed railway systems are much more complicated than the urban rail transit systems and some distinctions are shown in Table 1. In the view of functions, the VOBC includes Automatic Train Operation (ATO) and Automatic Train Protection (ATP), but there is no ATO for high speed railways. For trackside subsystems, the RBC and the ZC almost plays the same role, which is to generate and transmit the MA information. One big difference is the main method of train-ground wireless communica- tions, where the high speed railway is using GSM-R and the urban rail transit is using WLAN or other COTS technologies. The railway line is usually divided into areas or regions. Each area is under the control of a ZC or RBC and has its own wireless transmission Fig. 1. content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: [22]. Specifi- cally, it shows different features when the CBTC technologies are used respectively in high-speed railways and urban rail transit systems. The high-speed railway systems apply GSM-R as the primary method of train-ground commu- nications while the WLANs are adopted in urban rail transit systems. According to the analysis on GSM-R [23], there is no end to end encryption in GSM-R protocols, which means the wireless link between Mobile Stations (MS) and Base Stations (BS) is encrypted while the information between BS and BS is transmitted in clear texts. What’s more, the GSM- R uses one-way authentication, which means the network (BS) needs to authenticate the MS when it tries to access but there is no the same authentication process when the BS tries to communication with the MS. As a result, some active attacks can effi- ciently steal information transmitted in the GSM-R network in the form of false BS and make the network collapse. As WLANs work at the public frequency band, the urban rail transit systems face the same security risks compared with high-speed railway systems. Firstly, the interference is unavoidable, which has attracted the attention of subway operators and some research has been done [24–26]. Secondly, as the 802.11 protocols are not originally designed for railways, it shows some problems when WLANs are applied as the main method of train-ground communications, such as the handoff mechanisms [21]. Above all, the working frequency of WLANs is high and the coverage of one AP is small, which is 300500 m. As a result, the handoff happens frequently when the train travels with the high speed, and it provides more opportunities for attackers to access the network with the deficiency of WLAN handoff mechanisms. In fact, there are various WLAN products of different manufactures in urban rail transit systems, including Cisco, Simens, H3C and so on. Because of the im- perfection of 802.11 protocols, there is big difference between different products and it brings much more security issues for CBTC systems. However, as the CBTC systems could also be considered as a typical cyber physical system [27], the CBTC system could be divided into two parts: cyber space and physical space. According to the ANSI/ISA 99 standards, a reference model for ICS systems is proposed, and we build the same one based on the architecture of CBTC systems as shown in Fig. 2. content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: Other COTS Technologies system. Each train transmits its identity (ID), position, direction and ve- locity to the ZC (RBC). The continuous wireless link between each train and the ZC (RBC) can ensure that the ZC (RBC) knows the status in- formation of all train in its area all the time. Each train computes a braking curve with the MA which is generated based on the location of the train (or some obstacles) in front of it. Therefore, the current train can stop before it reaches the front train in order to keep safe. Theoretically, as long as each train is traveling at the same speed and have the same braking capability, they can travel as close as a few meters between them. However, the unreliable wireless communications can lead to commu- nication interruptions and long latency in the view of performance which is the current key research of the train-ground communications [21]. Due to the characteristics of wireless communications, the security issues should be one more important point, which could result in safety-related accidents. Besides the security vulnerabilities of wireless communications, there are more and more potential vulnerabilities from the application software, the operation systems, the hardware platforms with the wide application of COTS IT technologies. 2.2. The security situation of CBTC systems Compared with the traditional industrial control systems, the characteristics of CBTC systems could be summarized as follows: • The application of wireless communications brings a more efficient CBTC system. However, it is possible for malicious attackers to il- legal access the control network, which could bring unpredictable effects that are hard to be assessed. • The railway networks are deployed at the wide area across the country which means it offers high opportunities for attackers, and the probabilities of security attacks on CBTC systems are high, especially with the application of open wireless communications. • The COTS technology is widely used in CBTC systems, which is not originally designed for railways. As a result, the security issues are not considered. • The transmission protocols are not verified through security as- sessment, which means some potential vulnerabilities could be used by malicious attackers, and it can result in catastrophic accidents. As mentioned above, CBTC systems rely on the wireless commu- nications between trains and the ground control center [22]. Specifi- cally, it shows different features when the CBTC technologies are used respectively in high-speed railways and urban rail transit systems. The high-speed railway systems apply GSM-R as the primary method of train-ground commu- nic

content.title: Modeling of RAILWAY LogicS for Reverse Engineering, Verification and Refactoring
content.abstract_or_snippet: F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) 77–94 MODElINg OF RAIlWAY lOgIcS FOR REVERSE ENgINEERINg, VERIFIcATION AND REFAcTORINg F. FlAMMINI1,2, A. lAZZARO1 & N. MAZZOccA2 1ANSAlDO STS, RAMS Unit, Via Nuova delle Brecce 260, Naples 80147, Italy. 2University of Naples “Federico II”, Dipartimento di Informatica e Sistemistica, Via claudio 21, Naples 80125, Italy. ABSTRAcT Model-based approaches are widespread both in functional and non-functional verification activities of critical computer-based systems. Reverse engineering can also be used to support checks for correctness of system implementation against its requirements. In this paper, we show how a model-based technique, using the Unified Modeling language (UMl), suits the reverse engineering of complex control logics. UMl is usually exploited to drive the development of software systems, using an object-oriented and bottom-up approach; however, it can be also used to model legacy non-object-oriented logic processes featuring a clear distinction between data structures and related operations. Our case-study consists in the most important component of the European Railway Traffic Management System/European Train control System: the Radio Block center (RBc). The model we obtained from the logic code of the RBc significantly facilitated both structural and behavioral analyses, giving a valuable contribution to the static verification and refactoring of the software under test. Keywords: control software, modeling, railways, refactoring, reverse engineering, verification. 1 INTRODUcTION AND BAckgROUND A large class of fault tolerant systems is constituted by mission and safety critical apparels employed in industrial control applications, like defense, aerospace and transportation. In fact, most control systems nowadays feature complex computer-based architectures. critical control systems require a number of thorough verification and validation activities, which are regulated by international standards (see for instance [1, 2]). With reference to software veri- fication, such activities can be roughly divided into static and dynamic ones: static analyses do not require code execution, while dynamic ones require execution on the target hardware or in proper simulation environments. Theoretically, static analyses should precede dynamic ones in the system life cycle, with

content.title: Modeling of RAILWAY LogicS for Reverse Engineering, Verification and Refactoring
content.abstract_or_snippet: in the language of dynamic binding, pointers or references; in fact, the language was specifically developed to be understandable by non-programmers. The result is that the reverse engi- neering procedure can easily be automated, in analogy with computer Aided Software Engineering (cASE) tools featuring ‘round-trip’ engineering facilities suitable for general purpose languages (see e.g. [15]). Differently from such tools, the representation provided by the proprietary tool we are presently developing is application specific, that is to say it provides a representation fitting the specific needs of test engineers (a general and difficult to check representation would not prove useful for verification purposes). Next section will provide an example application of the model-based reverse engineering approach to the RBc case-study. 4.2 Reverse engineering, static verification and refactoring of the RBc In this section, the modeling technique from RBc logic language to UMl diagrams is described by referring to the simple ‘change of Traction Power’ (cTP) logic process. To give an idea of its relative complexity, such process is nearly 20 times smaller in terms of lines of code with respect to the MA process (the latter being very difficult to describe in detail with- out an in depth knowledge of the RBc software architecture). The cTP process has to manage manual commands of activation/deactivation of a change of traction power line section coming from the RBc operator. The activation/deactivation commands must be accepted only if the track circuit in which there is a change of power is free and not included in a MA assigned to a train. In the following we present the UMl based modeling and analysis for such example process. 4.2.1 Use-case diagrams Use-case diagrams represent how a logic process can be used by application processes or by the operator using manual commands; therefore, they represent the externally triggered 88 F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) high-level procedures (see Fig. 5). Usually, at least one use-case diagram is specified for each system level scenario (e.g. Start of Mission, Hand-Over, etc.). Use-case diagrams are significant in representing all the possible functional scenarios involving the logic process, hence giving the possibility to verifiers to check the validity of the actions performed on the process at a very high abstraction level; however, they reveal not so useful for refactoring purposes. 4.2.2 class diagrams class diagrams

content.title: Modeling of RAILWAY LogicS for Reverse Engineering, Verification and Refactoring
content.abstract_or_snippet: illegal or incompatible with process status, in order to tolerate casual (e.g. soft-errors in memories) as well as possibly systematic faults. Moreover, refactoring can involve the grouping of condition checks, the re-ordering of checks (weighted by occurrence probability and/or by the number of necessary steps to perform the check of the condition) and other specific performance optimizations. The use of sequence diagrams allows test engineers to precisely weigh the condition checks in terms of needed interactions between logic processes, and thus in terms of elaboration cycles. As for the respect of object-orientation, whenever necessary, operations must be added in class diagrams in order to let processes modify variables of other processes only by using specific methods and not by directly accessing external attributes. Such a modification also allows moving defensive programming controls from the calling methods to the newly added ones, possibly gaining in reliability, readability and code length. The behavioral impact of such modifications can be easily checked by means of UMl sequence and state diagrams. In particular, state diagrams also allow for behavioral refine- ments, e.g. aimed at moving/grouping states in composite ones. As compliance with high-level requirements has already been verified in the previous step, it is necessary that such transfor- mations preserve the logic behavior of the system. 4 AN INDUSTRIAl APPlIcATION: THE RADIO BlOck cENTER In our experience with the RBc, almost all the activities described in Section 3 were per- formed by hand; in fact, while the building and verification of the model is theoretically automatable, when the verification process started we were not completely confident about the worth of automating it (see Section 5 for future developments). As aforementioned, the structure of RBc logic processes well suits to be modeled using an object-based language: logic processes can be easily thought of as classes, with their internal ‘variables’ constituting the attributes, and their ‘commands’ being the operations. In particular, the ‘process status’ variable has been represented in a specific state diagram, as it triggers most of the process F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) 85 behavior and thus is very critical. The modeling tool we used to build UMl diagrams was the Rational Rose Modeler Edition [9]. While building the model we exploited all the syntactic controls and automations of the Rational Rose Modeler application, which constituted a first congruity check on the model under construction

updated_hypotheses.statement: In ETCS/ERTMS Level 2 ist ein RBC typischerweise nur für einen begrenzten geografischen/streckenseitigen Zuständigkeitsbereich ausgelegt; dadurch sind Schnittstellen zu benachbarten RBCs sowie formalisierte Train-Hand-Over-Prozeduren erforderlich, um durchgehende Zugfahrten über RBC-Grenzen zu ermöglichen.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Gute semantische Passung: Dokument beschreibt explizit, dass ein RBC „a limited number of track sections“ verwaltet und deshalb mit adjacent RBCs verbunden sein muss, um Train Hand-Over über RBC-Grenzen zu ermöglichen. Zusätzlich beschreibt Survey die Aufteilung der Linie in Areas/Regions unter Kontrolle eines RBC/ZC, was die Gebietszuständigkeit stützt. Keine Gegenbelege in den bereitgestellten Dokumenten.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Stand der Technik (Implementierungsarchitektur) für RBC-Software umfasst eine modulare Prozess-Architektur mit (a) Application-Prozessen in einer Safe-Subset-C-Implementierung für externe Interaktionen (u.a. Zugfunk/Train interaction, Interlocking/IXL interaction, Operator) und (b) separaten Logic-Prozessen in einer applikationsspezifischen Logiksprache, die durch einen Logic Manager interpretiert und geschedult werden; zentrale Logikprozesse prüfen z.B. die Integrität der Movement Authority anhand Strecken-/Belegungsbedingungen aus dem Interlocking.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Dokument liefert eine konkrete (wenn auch an einem Fallbeispiel erläuterte) Implementierungsarchitektur: Application-Prozesse in Safe-Subset-C für externe Interaktionen und Logic-Prozesse in applikationsspezifischer Logiksprache, interpretiert und gescheduled durch Logic Manager. Das deckt die Hypothese in Tiefe/Spezifizität gut ab. Einschränkung: Evidenz ist primär eine Quelle (2011) und beschreibt eine konkrete RBC-Architektur, nicht notwendigerweise den gesamten „Stand der Technik“ heute.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: RBC-Software wird in der Praxis gezielt performanzoptimiert (ohne unmittelbare Safety-Auswirkung), indem Prüfbedingungen in der Logik refaktoriert (Gruppierung/Umordnung) werden; solche Optimierungen können die Anzahl der vom RBC gleichzeitig handhabbaren Züge signifikant erhöhen (Größenordnung ~30% in einem industriellen Fallbeispiel).
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Fallbeispiel belegt direkt Performance-Optimierung durch Umordnung/Gruppierung von Condition-Checks und berichtet „final gain … about the 30%“ bzgl. Anzahl gemanagter Züge. Ebenso wird begründet, dass Trackside-Verzögerungen primär Performance betreffen, da Onboard via Vitality Monitor/Brakes reagiert; damit passt auch die Safety-Abgrenzung. Generalisierbarkeit bleibt unklar (ein industrielles Beispiel, spezifischer Kontext).

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Ein aktueller Entwicklungs- und Forschungsfokus für RBC-nahe Systeme liegt in der Absicherung und Leistungsfähigkeit der Zug-Funkkommunikation (insb. bei GSM-R): fehlende Ende-zu-Ende-Verschlüsselung und einseitige Authentisierung ermöglichen aktive Angriffe; zugleich werden Kommunikationsunterbrechungen und Latenzen als zentrales Performance-Problem der Train-Ground-Links diskutiert.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Survey stützt die Teilbehauptungen zu GSM-R-Schwächen (keine E2E-Verschlüsselung, one-way authentication) und nennt Kommunikationsunterbrechung/Latenz als aktuelles Key-Research-Thema. Der Bezug zu „RBC-nahen Systemen“ ist jedoch indirekt und im Dokument im CBTC/HSR-Kontext formuliert; es wird nicht spezifisch gezeigt, dass dies ein RBC-Entwicklungsfokus (RBC-Software/Produktentwicklung) ist, eher ein allgemeiner Train-Ground-Link-Fokus. Daher nur gemischter Fit.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

challenge_findings.description: Evidenzbasis für „Stand der Technik“ ist schmal (im Wesentlichen eine Quelle, zudem 2011) und kann heutige RBC-Softwarearchitekturen nur eingeschränkt repräsentieren (z.B. Virtualisierung, SIL4-Plattformen, Microservices, EULYNX/standardisierte Schnittstellen).

Die berichtete ~30%-Steigerung ist ein einzelnes industrielles Fallbeispiel; Übertragbarkeit auf andere RBCs/Lastprofile ist nicht belegt. Zudem ist die Optimierung teilweise auch strukturell (Einführung neuer Prozesse wie „Track Section“), nicht nur reine Umordnung von Checks.

Die Dokumente belegen Schwächen von GSM-R und Forschungsbedarf zu Latenz/Unterbrechungen, aber nicht, dass dies spezifisch ein aktueller Entwicklungsfokus der RBC-Technologie selbst ist (vs. allgemeine Train-Ground-Kommunikation/CBTC-Security).
Mehrere Dokumente stützen die Gebietszuständigkeit von RBCs und die Notwendigkeit von RBC-zu-RBC Hand-Over zur Ermöglichung durchgehender Fahrten über Area-Grenzen.
Fit score below threshold; broaden supporting evidence base.

content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: VC/VOBC, RBC/ZC, CI are the critical parts, which means the CBTC security testbed should adopt the real signalling equipment. However, the train can use an emulation product, which can generate the kinetic model and provide the comprehensive para- meters of the train for the control equipment. 6. Conclusions and future work CBTC is the most popular technology for railway transportations and facing the more and more serious security situation. As one typical industrial control system and the critical infrastructure, the security risks of CBTC systems should be controlled and mitigated. Hence, finding the security risk propagation principles, identifying the treats and vulnerabilities, monitoring the security situation of CBTC systems, responding the risks and performing the proper corresponding strate- gies should be all realized. However, due to the closure and other features, the security protection works acted on the CBTC systems are limited. As there are few valuable researches about the railway security, based on the research results of traditional IT systems and industrial control systems, we summarized the significant security issues of CBTC systems. We began our discussion with an overview of the CBTC se- curity characteristics. In particular, the effects of security risks on the CBTC system are analyzed including the safety and the operation effi- ciency. We then discussed the possible potential attack and summarized the current popular security protection strategies including the securing communication, firewalls and intrusion detection/prevention. Finally, we exploited some challenges of CBTC security research, where the relationship between safety and security, resilience, security manage- ment and testbed were discussed. In summary, the research on CBTC security needs much more work and there is still a long way ahead to enhance the security level of CBTC systems due to some challenging issues. In the article, the state-of-the- art in CBTC security is reviewed from both the engineering and the 15 Z. Yu, H. Wang and F. Chen High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 6–17 research results as there is lack of enough theoretical supports for se- curity issues of railway systems. The challenges proposed in the paper can be beneficial for the future research of CBTC security. References [1] X. Yang, H. Yin, J. Wu, et al., Recognizing the critical stations in urban rail net- works: An analysis method based on the smart-card data, IEEE Intell. Trans. Sys. Mag. 11 (1) (2019) 29–35. [2]

Moreover, the RBc is usually able to manage a limited number of track sections, thus it must be connected to other adjacent RBcs in order to allow for the so-called train Hand-Over (HO). The Hand-Over is the complex procedure that manages the passage of a train from the area supervised by a RBc to the area of adjacent RBcs. In ERTMS level 2, the on-board and track- side communicate by the gSM-R radio network using the Euroradio protocol (see Fig. 3). The software architecture of the RBc is depicted in Fig. 3. It consists of some ‘application’ processes (written in a safe subset of the c programming language), which manage the inter- action of the RBc with the external entities (i.e. other subsystems and the RBc operator), and some ‘logic’ processes, which implement the control logic of the RBc; the latter are written in an application specific logic language, as already mentioned above. The logic Manager has to interpret logic language, translating it into executable code, and to schedule the logic processes. The shaded rectangles in Fig. 4 represent the logic processes, which are the target of our work. For instance, the ‘MA’ process, shown in Fig. 3, verifies the integrity of the Movement Authority assigned to the train against all the significant track and route conditions. Such conditions are received from the interlocking system by means of the ‘IXl interaction’ application process and are managed by the ‘Tc’ logic process, which stores Track circuit physical conditions in its internal variables. If the MA integrity verifica- tion fails (for instance a track circuit is not clear or involved in an emergency condition), the 86 F. Flammini, et al., Int. content.abstract_or_snippet: used to build UMl diagrams was the Rational Rose Modeler Edition [9]. While building the model we exploited all the syntactic controls and automations of the Rational Rose Modeler application, which constituted a first congruity check on the model under construction. Some examples: as the model grows up, the already defined links are automatically added by the application; in sequence diagrams, it is not possible to call an operation which has not been already defined in class diagrams; the accessibility of attributes and operations (public, protected, private) is automatically checked; etc. Next section will provide a general introduction to ERTMS/ETcS. 4.1 ERTMS/ETcS level 2 system implementation ERTMS/ETcS is the specification of a standard aiming at improving safety, performance and interoperability of European railways [7]. In Italy, the so-called level 2 specification of ERTMS/ETcS is implemented in high-speed railway lines. ERTMS/ETcS specifies three main subsystems: trackside, on-board and lineside. The trackside subsystem is the ground part of the overall system, and manages railway signaling to ensure safe train separation and rout- ing. The most important trackside subsystem is the Radio Block center, which has the aim of collecting track and train information in order to provide trains with necessary data: Move- ment Authorities (MA), that is the distance trains are authorized to move on; Static Speed Profiles (SSP), that is maximum speed limits allowed by the track; possible Emergency Stops (ES). The on-board control system performs train protection by controlling train speed against the elaborated dynamic speed profiles (or braking curves). The lineside is constituted by the so-called balises, which are devices positioned between the track lines, which have the aim to transmit geographical positioning data to the trains passing over them; such data is used by the on-board system in order to build and send position reports to the trackside. The Radio Block center is responsible of providing train headways by using data received in train position reports and delivering the correct MA, SSP and ES messages to the trains. In order to detect the necessary route and Track circuit (Tc) occupation status, RBc is con- nected to the national Interlocking (IXl) system, which is not standardized in ERTMS. Moreover, the RBc is usually able to manage a limited number of track sections, thus it must be connected to other adjacent RBcs in order to allow for the

J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) By checking sequence diagrams, we discovered that in some cases the controls on the feasibility of a manual command were missing. This was easy to visually check on the diagrams as well as to be automated by defining a rule according to which such control must be performed for each command received by a process. In particular, the check allowed to detect a missing control in the ES process causing the RBc to safely shut- down when the user tried to send an emergency message to a non-existing train number. Usually, such errors are revealed in the functional testing phase, while the approach described in this paper allowed test engineers to detect them earlier in system V&V proc- ess. Furthermore, by tracing sequence diagrams into functional requirements, we discovered some pieces of code related to never referenced operations, which were inher- ited from an early specification: an application of refactoring, in this case, consisted in removing such pleonastic operations and attributes. Sequence diagrams also prove very useful for refactoring, of which we present in the following a quite general example. From the analysis of sequence diagrams, we realized that most of the RBc logic code was made up by condition verification statements. Such statements are clearly explicated in sequence diagrams, which also show their expected weight in terms of process interactions and thus of elaboration cycles (given the fixed policy, each interaction with an already scheduled process requires a further elaboration cycle). For instance, ‘lighter’ condition verifications only need an access to one or more internal attributes; ‘heavier’ ones involve the interac- tion of two or more processes, and can last several elaboration cycles in the fixed process scheduling scheme. Therefore, by properly grouping and shifting conditions we were able to predict and minimize the average number of controls performed by the RBc, with a significant improvement in system performance, which directly impacted on the number of trains the RBc was able to manage (final gain was about the 30%). For instance, if a Tc is involved in an emergency condition, there is no need to check its occupation status: as stated by the functional requirements, immediately after the emergency

content.title: Modeling of RAILWAY LogicS for Reverse Engineering, Verification and Refactoring
content.abstract_or_snippet: RBc was able to manage (final gain was about the 30%). For instance, if a Tc is involved in an emergency condition, there is no need to check its occupation status: as stated by the functional requirements, immediately after the emergency is detected, an emergency message must be sent to the train. Thus, it would be natural to set such control as the first to be performed. However, the probability of an emergency is very low, and such a check would be false most of the times, wasting elaboration time. Therefore, start- ing from the consideration that an emergency must always include all the Tc of a track section (at least 3), a more effective design review consisted in adding a new process named Track Section and making the MA process perform a single check on this process instead of performing it for each Tc. Please note that real-time constraint are not taken into account in the specific application (ERTMS/ETcS trackside subsystem) as the on-board system is responsible for reacting to excessive delays in receiving messages from the RBc. The reaction consists in the applica- tion of service or emergency brakes activated by a proper channel vitality monitor, as specified by the standard, which also protects from loss of radio packets due to gSM-R unavailability. Therefore, the delay in sending messages by the trackside subsystem only has an impact on system performance (which is tested apart) but not on its safety. 4.2.4 State diagrams State diagrams show the transitions of the state variables of the logic processes against the possible triggering events. The cTP state diagram shown in Fig. 8 has been simplified to be self-explaining. By checking the diagram it is immediately clear that for the cTP process all input conditions at any state have been considered, and there are no unreachable or deadlock states (which have been detected and corrected for some other processes). In the design phase, the state machine of each process of the Radio Block center has been kept enough F. Flammini, et al., Int. Therefore, the security issue of CBTC systems are present. 2.1. A typical CBTC system The aim of CBTC is to improve the efficiency of train operation through the application of wireless communications, and the level of system safety should also be enhanced. Fig. 1 describes a typical CBTC system, which consists of on-board subsystems, Data Communication Systems (DCS) and trackside subsystems. The trackside subsystems are connected through the wired (Ethernet) network, which provide the movement. The Movement Authority (MA) for on-board systems through the wireless network. Similarly, the information of trains including the current location and the speed is transmitted to the trackside subsystems which helps to calculate the MA. Although the same system architecture is ap- plied, the high speed railway systems are much more complicated than the urban rail transit systems and some distinctions are shown in Table 1. In the view of functions, the VOBC includes Automatic Train Operation (ATO) and Automatic Train Protection (ATP), but there is no ATO for high speed railways. For trackside subsystems, the RBC and the ZC almost plays the same role, which is to generate and transmit the MA information. One big difference is the main method of train-ground wireless communica- tions, where the high speed railway is using GSM-R and the urban rail transit is using WLAN or other COTS technologies. The railway line is usually divided into areas or regions. Each area is under the control of a ZC or RBC and has its own wireless transmission Fig. 1. content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: [22]. Specifi- cally, it shows different features when the CBTC technologies are used respectively in high-speed railways and urban rail transit systems. The high-speed railway systems apply GSM-R as the primary method of train-ground commu- nications while the WLANs are adopted in urban rail transit systems. According to the analysis on GSM-R [23], there is no end to end encryption in GSM-R protocols, which means the wireless link between Mobile Stations (MS) and Base Stations (BS) is encrypted while the information between BS and BS is transmitted in clear texts. What’s more, the GSM- R uses one-way authentication, which means the network (BS) needs to authenticate the MS when it tries to access but there is no the same authentication process when the BS tries to communication with the MS. As a result, some active attacks can effi- ciently steal information transmitted in the GSM-R network in the form of false BS and make the network collapse. As WLANs work at the public frequency band, the urban rail transit systems face the same security risks compared with high-speed railway systems. Firstly, the interference is unavoidable, which has attracted the attention of subway operators and some research has been done [24–26]. Secondly, as the 802.11 protocols are not originally designed for railways, it shows some problems when WLANs are applied as the main method of train-ground communications, such as the handoff mechanisms [21]. Above all, the working frequency of WLANs is high and the coverage of one AP is small, which is 300500 m. As a result, the handoff happens frequently when the train travels with the high speed, and it provides more opportunities for attackers to access the network with the deficiency of WLAN handoff mechanisms. In fact, there are various WLAN products of different manufactures in urban rail transit systems, including Cisco, Simens, H3C and so on. Because of the im- perfection of 802.11 protocols, there is big difference between different products and it brings much more security issues for CBTC systems. However, as the CBTC systems could also be considered as a typical cyber physical system [27], the CBTC system could be divided into two parts: cyber space and physical space. According to the ANSI/ISA 99 standards, a reference model for ICS systems is proposed, and we build the same one based on the architecture of CBTC systems as shown in Fig. 2. For CBTC systems, the Centralized Traffic Control (CT

content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: Other COTS Technologies system. Each train transmits its identity (ID), position, direction and ve- locity to the ZC (RBC). The continuous wireless link between each train and the ZC (RBC) can ensure that the ZC (RBC) knows the status in- formation of all train in its area all the time. Each train computes a braking curve with the MA which is generated based on the location of the train (or some obstacles) in front of it. Therefore, the current train can stop before it reaches the front train in order to keep safe. Theoretically, as long as each train is traveling at the same speed and have the same braking capability, they can travel as close as a few meters between them. However, the unreliable wireless communications can lead to commu- nication interruptions and long latency in the view of performance which is the current key research of the train-ground communications [21]. Due to the characteristics of wireless communications, the security issues should be one more important point, which could result in safety-related accidents. Besides the security vulnerabilities of wireless communications, there are more and more potential vulnerabilities from the application software, the operation systems, the hardware platforms with the wide application of COTS IT technologies. 2.2. The security situation of CBTC systems Compared with the traditional industrial control systems, the characteristics of CBTC systems could be summarized as follows: • The application of wireless communications brings a more efficient CBTC system. However, it is possible for malicious attackers to il- legal access the control network, which could bring unpredictable effects that are hard to be assessed. • The railway networks are deployed at the wide area across the country which means it offers high opportunities for attackers, and the probabilities of security attacks on CBTC systems are high, especially with the application of open wireless communications. • The COTS technology is widely used in CBTC systems, which is not originally designed for railways. As a result, the security issues are not considered. • The transmission protocols are not verified through security as- sessment, which means some potential vulnerabilities could be used by malicious attackers, and it can result in catastrophic accidents. As mentioned above, CBTC systems rely on the wireless commu- nications between trains and the ground control center [22]. Specifi- cally, it shows different features when the CBTC technologies are used respectively in high-speed railways and urban rail transit systems. The high-speed railway systems apply GSM-R as the primary method of train-ground commu- nic

content.abstract_or_snippet: F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) 77–94 MODElINg OF RAIlWAY lOgIcS FOR REVERSE ENgINEERINg, VERIFIcATION AND REFAcTORINg F. FlAMMINI1,2, A. lAZZARO1 & N. MAZZOccA2 1ANSAlDO STS, RAMS Unit, Via Nuova delle Brecce 260, Naples 80147, Italy. 2University of Naples “Federico II”, Dipartimento di Informatica e Sistemistica, Via claudio 21, Naples 80125, Italy. ABSTRAcT Model-based approaches are widespread both in functional and non-functional verification activities of critical computer-based systems. Reverse engineering can also be used to support checks for correctness of system implementation against its requirements. In this paper, we show how a model-based technique, using the Unified Modeling language (UMl), suits the reverse engineering of complex control logics. UMl is usually exploited to drive the development of software systems, using an object-oriented and bottom-up approach; however, it can be also used to model legacy non-object-oriented logic processes featuring a clear distinction between data structures and related operations. Our case-study consists in the most important component of the European Railway Traffic Management System/European Train control System: the Radio Block center (RBc). The model we obtained from the logic code of the RBc significantly facilitated both structural and behavioral analyses, giving a valuable contribution to the static verification and refactoring of the software under test. Keywords: control software, modeling, railways, refactoring, reverse engineering, verification. 1 INTRODUcTION AND BAckgROUND A large class of fault tolerant systems is constituted by mission and safety critical apparels employed in industrial control applications, like defense, aerospace and transportation. In fact, most control systems nowadays feature complex computer-based architectures. critical control systems require a number of thorough verification and validation activities, which are regulated by international standards (see for instance [1, 2]). With reference to software veri- fication, such activities can be roughly divided into static and dynamic ones: static analyses do not require code execution, while dynamic ones require execution on the target hardware or in proper simulation environments. Theoretically, static analyses should precede dynamic ones in the system life cycle, with

content.abstract_or_snippet: in the language of dynamic binding, pointers or references; in fact, the language was specifically developed to be understandable by non-programmers. The result is that the reverse engi- neering procedure can easily be automated, in analogy with computer Aided Software Engineering (cASE) tools featuring ‘round-trip’ engineering facilities suitable for general purpose languages (see e.g. [15]). Differently from such tools, the representation provided by the proprietary tool we are presently developing is application specific, that is to say it provides a representation fitting the specific needs of test engineers (a general and difficult to check representation would not prove useful for verification purposes). Next section will provide an example application of the model-based reverse engineering approach to the RBc case-study. 4.2 Reverse engineering, static verification and refactoring of the RBc In this section, the modeling technique from RBc logic language to UMl diagrams is described by referring to the simple ‘change of Traction Power’ (cTP) logic process. To give an idea of its relative complexity, such process is nearly 20 times smaller in terms of lines of code with respect to the MA process (the latter being very difficult to describe in detail with- out an in depth knowledge of the RBc software architecture). The cTP process has to manage manual commands of activation/deactivation of a change of traction power line section coming from the RBc operator. The activation/deactivation commands must be accepted only if the track circuit in which there is a change of power is free and not included in a MA assigned to a train. In the following we present the UMl based modeling and analysis for such example process. 4.2.1 Use-case diagrams Use-case diagrams represent how a logic process can be used by application processes or by the operator using manual commands; therefore, they represent the externally triggered 88 F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) high-level procedures (see Fig. 5). Usually, at least one use-case diagram is specified for each system level scenario (e.g. Start of Mission, Hand-Over, etc.). Use-case diagrams are significant in representing all the possible functional scenarios involving the logic process, hence giving the possibility to verifiers to check the validity of the actions performed on the process at a very high abstraction level; however, they reveal not so useful for refactoring purposes. 4.2.2 class diagrams class diagrams

content.title: Modeling of RAILWAY LogicS for Reverse Engineering, Verification and Refactoring
content.abstract_or_snippet: illegal or incompatible with process status, in order to tolerate casual (e.g. soft-errors in memories) as well as possibly systematic faults. Moreover, refactoring can involve the grouping of condition checks, the re-ordering of checks (weighted by occurrence probability and/or by the number of necessary steps to perform the check of the condition) and other specific performance optimizations. The use of sequence diagrams allows test engineers to precisely weigh the condition checks in terms of needed interactions between logic processes, and thus in terms of elaboration cycles. As for the respect of object-orientation, whenever necessary, operations must be added in class diagrams in order to let processes modify variables of other processes only by using specific methods and not by directly accessing external attributes. Such a modification also allows moving defensive programming controls from the calling methods to the newly added ones, possibly gaining in reliability, readability and code length. The behavioral impact of such modifications can be easily checked by means of UMl sequence and state diagrams. In particular, state diagrams also allow for behavioral refine- ments, e.g. aimed at moving/grouping states in composite ones. As compliance with high-level requirements has already been verified in the previous step, it is necessary that such transfor- mations preserve the logic behavior of the system. 4 AN INDUSTRIAl APPlIcATION: THE RADIO BlOck cENTER In our experience with the RBc, almost all the activities described in Section 3 were per- formed by hand; in fact, while the building and verification of the model is theoretically automatable, when the verification process started we were not completely confident about the worth of automating it (see Section 5 for future developments). As aforementioned, the structure of RBc logic processes well suits to be modeled using an object-based language: logic processes can be easily thought of as classes, with their internal ‘variables’ constituting the attributes, and their ‘commands’ being the operations. In particular, the ‘process status’ variable has been represented in a specific state diagram, as it triggers most of the process F. Flammini, et al., Int. J. of Safety and Security Eng., Vol. 1, No. 1 (2011) 85 behavior and thus is very critical. The modeling tool we used to build UMl diagrams was the Rational Rose Modeler Edition [9]. While building the model we exploited all the syntactic controls and automations of the Rational Rose Modeler application, which constituted a first congruity check on the model under construction

Die Dokumente belegen Schwächen von GSM-R und Forschungsbedarf zu Latenz/Unterbrechungen, aber nicht, dass dies spezifisch ein aktueller Entwicklungsfokus der RBC-Technologie selbst ist (vs. allgemeine Train-Ground-Kommunikation/CBTC-Security).

synthesis_output.answer:
## Kurzfazit: aktueller Entwicklungsstand von RBC
Ein belastbarer „aktueller“ Entwicklungsstand (z. B. Stand der ETCS-Baselines/Subset-Standards, heutige Produktgenerationen, Virtualisierung/Cloud-RBC, FRMCS-Integration, aktuelle Zulassungs-/Cybersecurity-Anforderungen) ist mit den vorliegenden Quellen nicht direkt belegbar. Die Quellen zeigen jedoch, dass die RBC-Technologie als zentrales ETCS/ERTMS-Level-2-Trackside-Element seit mindestens 2011 in industriellen Implementierungen mit klarer Softwarearchitektur, definierter Rollenverteilung (MA/SSP/ES), Hand-Over-Mechanismen zwischen benachbarten RBCs und systematischer V&V/Refactoring-Praxis beschrieben ist. Zusätzlich wird für funknahen Train-Ground-Link (GSM-R) ein sicherheits- und performancegetriebener Forschungsbedarf (fehlende Ende-zu-Ende-Verschlüsselung, einseitige Authentisierung, Latenz/Unterbrechungen) herausgearbeitet, allerdings im CBTC/HSR-Kontext und nicht als spezifischer RBC-Produktentwicklungsstand. [3][4][5][6][8][9][10]

## Rolle und Funktionsumfang des RBC im ETCS/ERTMS-Level-2-System
Der RBC wird als „wichtigste“ Komponente des Trackside-Subsystems in ERTMS/ETCS Level 2 beschrieben und sammelt Track- und Zuginformationen, um den Zügen zentrale Signalisierungsdaten zu liefern: Movement Authorities (MA), Static Speed Profiles (SSP) und Emergency Stops (ES). [4]
Die Sicherung der Zugfolge (Headways) erfolgt durch Auswertung von Daten aus Positionsmeldungen der Züge und anschließende Übermittlung korrekter MA/SSP/ES-Nachrichten an die Züge. [4]
Für die Streckenortung nutzt das System Balisen, deren Daten vom On-board-System zur Erstellung von Positionsreports für das Trackside-System verwendet werden. [4]

## Systemkopplungen und Gebietszuschnitt: RBC-Hand-Over als Reifeindikator
Ein RBC verwaltet typischerweise nur eine begrenzte Anzahl von Streckenabschnitten (track sections). Deshalb muss er mit benachbarten RBCs verbunden werden, um Train-Hand-Over (HO) zu ermöglichen – eine „komplexe Prozedur“ für den Übergang eines Zuges von einem RBC-Zuständigkeitsbereich in den nächsten. [3][4]
Zusätzlich wird (im CBTC/HSR-Vergleich) beschrieben, dass Bahnlinien üblicherweise in Areas/Regions geteilt sind, die jeweils unter Kontrolle eines ZC oder RBC stehen; Züge übertragen Identität, Position, Richtung und Geschwindigkeit an ZC/RBC, wodurch der Status aller Züge im Bereich kontinuierlich bekannt ist. [8][10]

## Implementierungsarchitektur (industrielles Beispiel) und Engineering-Praxis
Für eine industrielle ETCS-Level-2-Trackside-Implementierung wird eine konkrete RBC-Softwarearchitektur beschrieben: 
- „Application“-Prozesse (in einem sicheren Subset von C) übernehmen die Interaktion des RBC mit externen Entitäten (andere Subsysteme sowie Operator). [3]
- „Logic“-Prozesse implementieren die Steuerlogik und sind in einer applikationsspezifischen Logiksprache realisiert; ein Logic Manager interpretiert diese Logiksprache, übersetzt sie in ausführbaren Code und schedult die Logic-Prozesse. [3]
- Ein zentrales Beispiel ist der MA-Prozess, der die Integrität einer Movement Authority gegen „significant track and route conditions“ prüft. Diese Bedingungen kommen aus dem Interlocking über einen eigenen Application-Prozess („IXL interaction“) und werden u. a. durch einen Tc-Logikprozess verwaltet, der Track-Circuit-Zustände in internen Variablen hält. [3]
Zum Entwicklungs-/V&V-Vorgehen zeigt ein industrielles Fallbeispiel modellbasiertes Reverse Engineering (UML) der RBC-Logikprozesse zur Unterstützung statischer Verifikation und Refactoring. 

## Performance-Optimierung und Safety-Abgrenzung (Fallbeispiel)
Im beschriebenen Fall wurden anhand von Sequenzdiagrammen fehlende Prüfungen (z. B. Feasibility-Checks für manuelle Kommandos) identifiziert und dadurch Fehler früher im V&V-Prozess gefunden (u. a. ein fehlender Check im ES-Prozess, der zu einem sicheren Shutdown bei ungültiger Train-ID führte). [5]
Zudem wird berichtet, dass durch Gruppierung und Verschiebung von Prüfbedingungen in der RBC-Logik die durchschnittliche Anzahl an Checks reduziert und die Performance signifikant gesteigert werden konnte; als Ergebnis habe sich die Anzahl der vom RBC handhabbaren Züge um etwa 30 % erhöht (industrielles Fallbeispiel). [5]
Dabei wird explizit argumentiert, dass Verzögerungen beim Senden von Trackside-Nachrichten im betrachteten ETCS-Level-2-Trackside-Kontext die Safety nicht direkt beeinträchtigen, da das On-board-System über einen Vitality Monitor auf ausbleibende Nachrichten reagiert (Service-/Notbremsung) und der Standard auch Paketverluste bei GSM-R-Unverfügbarkeit adressiert; Verzögerungen wirkten daher primär auf die Performance. [6]

## Kommunikations- und Cybersecurity-Themen im Umfeld von RBC/ZC
Für Systeme, in denen RBC/ZC die MA generieren und per drahtloser Kommunikation übertragen, werden Kommunikationsunterbrechungen und hohe Latenzen als aktuelles Performance-Problem der Train-Ground-Kommunikation diskutiert (als „current key research“). [10]
Für GSM-R wird (im Rahmen einer Sicherheitsanalyse) hervorgehoben:
- keine Ende-zu-Ende-Verschlüsselung (nur Funkstrecke MS–BS verschlüsselt; Teile im Kernnetz Klartext), [9]
- einseitige Authentisierung (BS authentisiert MS, umgekehrt nicht), was aktive Angriffe (z. B. falsche Basisstationen) begünstigen kann. [9]
Diese Aussagen sind als Umfeld-/Systemkontext zu verstehen; eine direkte Ableitung, dass dies bereits in der RBC-Technologie selbst (z. B. RBC-Produktfeatures) umgesetzt ist, ist hieraus nicht möglich. [9][10]

## Einordnung der Evidenzlage
Die detailliertesten RBC-Aussagen stammen aus einem einzelnen, älteren industriellen Fallbeispiel (2011) und belegen Architektur, Hand-Over-Notwendigkeit sowie V&V-/Refactoring- und Performance-Aspekte, aber nicht den heutigen Standard- und Produktstand. [3][4][5][6]
Die neuere Quelle (2023) adressiert RBC primär im CBTC/HSR-Kontext und liefert v. a. Security-/Kommunikationsaspekte zu GSM-R bzw. Wireless Links, nicht jedoch ETCS-Baselines, RBC-Produktgenerationen oder Zulassungsstatus. [8][9][10][1]

## Schlussfolgerung zum „aktuellen Entwicklungsstand“ (streng quellengestützt)
- RBC ist funktional klar als Trackside-Instanz zur Erzeugung/Übermittlung von MA/SSP/ES und zur Headway-Bereitstellung beschrieben. [4]
- RBC-Deployments sind in Zuständigkeitsbereiche segmentiert; Inter-RBC-Hand-Over ist erforderlich und als komplexe Prozedur implementierungsrelevant. [3][8]
- In industriellen Implementierungen existieren (mindestens) modulare Prozessarchitekturen (Application/Logic, Safe-Subset-C + domänenspezifische Logiksprache, Logic Manager) und etablierte modellbasierte V&V-/Refactoring-Methoden. [3]
- Leistungsfähigkeit/Skalierung ist ein Entwicklungsgegenstand; es sind signifikante Kapazitätsgewinne durch refactoringbasierte Optimierung berichtet (Fallbeispiel, ~30%). [5]
- Funkkommunikation (GSM-R) zeigt dokumentierte Security- und Performance-Herausforderungen, die für RBC/Zug-Kommunikationsketten relevant sind, aber nicht als konkreter RBC-Technologie-Release-Stand belegt sind. [9][10]

Missing information:
- Aktuelle normative Referenz zum RBC in ETCS/ERTMS (z. B. Subset-026, Baseline 3/4, Schnittstellen-/Nachrichtenspezifikationen wie Euroradio, RBC-RBC/IXL-Interfaces) inkl. Versions-/Änderungsstand ist nicht belegt.
- Konkreter Markt-/Produktstand heutiger RBC-Lösungen (Lieferanten, Releases, Funktionsumfang wie ATO over ETCS, Schnittstellenstandardisierung, Redundanz/Failover) ist nicht belegt.
- Nachweise zu Virtualisierung/Cloud-native/distributed RBC-Architekturen und deren Reifegrad/Zulassung sind nicht belegt.
- Aktueller Stand zu Safety Case/Zertifizierung (SIL4, EN 50126/50128/50129) für RBC in aktuellen Projekten ist nicht belegt; es gibt nur allgemeinen Standardverweis im Kontext Safety-Critical-Entwicklung.
- Spezifische RBC-bezogene Cybersecurity-Anforderungen/Standards (z. B. Euroradio-Weiterentwicklung, ETCS over IP, FRMCS-Migration) und deren Umsetzungsstand in RBCs sind nicht belegt.

Aufgestellte Hypothesen:
Hypothese 1: In ETCS/ERTMS Level 2 ist ein RBC typischerweise nur für einen begrenzten geografischen/streckenseitigen Zuständigkeitsbereich ausgelegt; dadurch sind Schnittstellen zu benachbarten RBCs sowie formalisierte Train-Hand-Over-Prozeduren erforderlich, um durchgehende Zugfahrten über RBC-Grenzen zu ermöglichen.
Reasoning: fit=0.65, semantic=inferential, sources=2, diversity=0.60, contradiction=False
Supporting evidence document IDs: [3], [7]

Zugfunk/Train interaction, Interlocking/IXL interaction, Operator) und (b) separaten Logic-Prozessen in einer applikationsspezifischen Logiksprache, die durch einen Logic Manager interpretiert und geschedult werden; zentrale Logikprozesse prüfen z.B. die Integrität der Movement Authority anhand Strecken-/Belegungsbedingungen aus dem Interlocking.
Reasoning: fit=0.58, semantic=tangential, sources=1, diversity=0.33, contradiction=False

Hypothese 3: RBC-Software wird in der Praxis gezielt performanzoptimiert (ohne unmittelbare Safety-Auswirkung), indem Prüfbedingungen in der Logik refaktoriert (Gruppierung/Umordnung) werden; solche Optimierungen können die Anzahl der vom RBC gleichzeitig handhabbaren Züge signifikant erhöhen (Größenordnung ~30% in einem industriellen Fallbeispiel).

synthesis_output.summary:
## Kurzfazit: aktueller Entwicklungsstand von RBC
Ein belastbarer „aktueller“ Entwicklungsstand (z. B. Stand der ETCS-Baselines/Subset-Standards, heutige Produktgenerationen, Virtualisierung/Cloud-RBC, FRMCS-Integration, aktuelle Zulassungs-/Cybersecurity-Anforderungen) ist mit den vorliegenden Quellen nicht direkt belegbar. Die Quellen zeigen jedoch, dass die RBC-Technologie als zentrales ETCS/ERTMS-Level-2-Trackside-Element seit mindestens 2011 in industriellen Implementierungen mit klarer Softwarearchitektur, definierter Rollenverteilung (MA/SSP/ES), Hand-Over-Mechanismen zwischen benachbarten RBCs und systematischer V&V/Refactoring-Praxis beschrieben ist. Zusätzlich wird für funknahen Train-Ground-Link (GSM-R) ein sicherheits- und performancegetriebener Forschungsbedarf (fehlende Ende-zu-Ende-Verschlüsselung, einseitige Authentisierung, Latenz/Unterbrechungen) herausgearbeitet, allerdings im CBTC/HSR-Kontext und nicht als spezifischer RBC-Produktentwicklungsstand. [3][4][5][6][8][9][10]

## Rolle und Funktionsumfang des RBC im ETCS/ERTMS-Level-2-System
Der RBC wird als „wichtigste“ Komponente des Trackside-Subsystems in ERTMS/ETCS Level 2 beschrieben und sammelt Track- und Zuginformationen, um den Zügen zentrale Signalisierungsdaten zu liefern: Movement Authorities (MA), Static Speed Profiles (SSP) und Emergency Stops (ES). [4]
Die Sicherung der Zugfolge (Headways) erfolgt durch Auswertung von Daten aus Positionsmeldungen der Züge und anschließende Übermittlung korrekter MA/SSP/ES-Nachrichten an die Züge. [4]
Für die Streckenortung nutzt das System Balisen, deren Daten vom On-board-System zur Erstellung von Positionsreports für das Trackside-System verwendet werden. [4]

## Systemkopplungen und Gebietszuschnitt: RBC-Hand-Over als Reifeindikator
Ein RBC verwaltet typischerweise nur eine begrenzte Anzahl von Streckenabschnitten (track sections). Deshalb muss er mit benachbarten RBCs verbunden werden, um Train-Hand-Over (HO) zu ermöglichen – eine „komplexe Prozedur“ für den Übergang eines Zuges von einem RBC-Zuständigkeitsbereich in den nächsten. [3][4]
Zusätzlich wird (im CBTC/HSR-Vergleich) beschrieben, dass Bahnlinien üblicherweise in Areas/Regions geteilt sind, die jeweils unter Kontrolle eines ZC oder RBC stehen; Züge übertragen Identität, Position, Richtung und Geschwindigkeit an ZC/RBC, wodurch der Status aller Züge im Bereich kontinuierlich bekannt ist. [8][10]

## Implementierungsarchitektur (industrielles Beispiel) und Engineering-Praxis
Für eine industrielle ETCS-Level-2-Trackside-Implementierung wird eine konkrete RBC-Softwarearchitektur beschrieben: 
- „Application“-Prozesse (in einem sicheren Subset von C) übernehmen die Interaktion des RBC mit externen Entitäten (andere Subsysteme sowie Operator). [3]
- „Logic“-Prozesse implementieren die Steuerlogik und sind in einer applikationsspezifischen Logiksprache realisiert; ein Logic Manager interpretiert diese Logiksprache, übersetzt sie in ausführbaren Code und schedult die Logic-Prozesse. [3]
- Ein zentrales Beispiel ist der MA-Prozess, der die Integrität einer Movement Authority gegen „significant track and route conditions“ prüft. Diese Bedingungen kommen aus dem Interlocking über einen eigenen Application-Prozess („IXL interaction“) und werden u. a. durch einen Tc-Logikprozess verwaltet, der Track-Circuit-Zustände in internen Variablen hält. [3]
Zum Entwicklungs-/V&V-Vorgehen zeigt ein industrielles Fallbeispiel modellbasiertes Reverse Engineering (UML) der RBC-Logikprozesse zur Unterstützung statischer Verifikation und Refactoring. 

## Performance-Optimierung und Safety-Abgrenzung (Fallbeispiel)
Im beschriebenen Fall wurden anhand von Sequenzdiagrammen fehlende Prüfungen (z. B. Feasibility-Checks für manuelle Kommandos) identifiziert und dadurch Fehler früher im V&V-Prozess gefunden (u. a. ein fehlender Check im ES-Prozess, der zu einem sicheren Shutdown bei ungültiger Train-ID führte). [5]
Zudem wird berichtet, dass durch Gruppierung und Verschiebung von Prüfbedingungen in der RBC-Logik die durchschnittliche Anzahl an Checks reduziert und die Performance signifikant gesteigert werden konnte; als Ergebnis habe sich die Anzahl der vom RBC handhabbaren Züge um etwa 30 % erhöht (industrielles Fallbeispiel). [5]
Dabei wird explizit argumentiert, dass Verzögerungen beim Senden von Trackside-Nachrichten im betrachteten ETCS-Level-2-Trackside-Kontext die Safety nicht direkt beeinträchtigen, da das On-board-System über einen Vitality Monitor auf ausbleibende Nachrichten reagiert (Service-/Notbremsung) und der Standard auch Paketverluste bei GSM-R-Unverfügbarkeit adressiert; Verzögerungen wirkten daher primär auf die Performance. [6]

## Kommunikations- und Cybersecurity-Themen im Umfeld von RBC/ZC
Für Systeme, in denen RBC/ZC die MA generieren und per drahtloser Kommunikation übertragen, werden Kommunikationsunterbrechungen und hohe Latenzen als aktuelles Performance-Problem der Train-Ground-Kommunikation diskutiert (als „current key research“). [10]
Für GSM-R wird (im Rahmen einer Sicherheitsanalyse) hervorgehoben:
- keine Ende-zu-Ende-Verschlüsselung (nur Funkstrecke MS–BS verschlüsselt; Teile im Kernnetz Klartext), [9]
- einseitige Authentisierung (BS authentisiert MS, umgekehrt nicht), was aktive Angriffe (z. B. falsche Basisstationen) begünstigen kann. [9]
Diese Aussagen sind als Umfeld-/Systemkontext zu verstehen; eine direkte Ableitung, dass dies bereits in der RBC-Technologie selbst (z. B. RBC-Produktfeatures) umgesetzt ist, ist hieraus nicht möglich. [9][10]

## Einordnung der Evidenzlage
Die detailliertesten RBC-Aussagen stammen aus einem einzelnen, älteren industriellen Fallbeispiel (2011) und belegen Architektur, Hand-Over-Notwendigkeit sowie V&V-/Refactoring- und Performance-Aspekte, aber nicht den heutigen Standard- und Produktstand. [3][4][5][6]
Die neuere Quelle (2023) adressiert RBC primär im CBTC/HSR-Kontext und liefert v. a. Security-/Kommunikationsaspekte zu GSM-R bzw. Wireless Links, nicht jedoch ETCS-Baselines, RBC-Produktgenerationen oder Zulassungsstatus. [8][9][10][1]

## Schlussfolgerung zum „aktuellen Entwicklungsstand“ (streng quellengestützt)
- RBC ist funktional klar als Trackside-Instanz zur Erzeugung/Übermittlung von MA/SSP/ES und zur Headway-Bereitstellung beschrieben. [4]
- RBC-Deployments sind in Zuständigkeitsbereiche segmentiert; Inter-RBC-Hand-Over ist erforderlich und als komplexe Prozedur implementierungsrelevant. [3][8]
- In industriellen Implementierungen existieren (mindestens) modulare Prozessarchitekturen (Application/Logic, Safe-Subset-C + domänenspezifische Logiksprache, Logic Manager) und etablierte modellbasierte V&V-/Refactoring-Methoden. [3]
- Leistungsfähigkeit/Skalierung ist ein Entwicklungsgegenstand; es sind signifikante Kapazitätsgewinne durch refactoringbasierte Optimierung berichtet (Fallbeispiel, ~30%). [5]
- Funkkommunikation (GSM-R) zeigt dokumentierte Security- und Performance-Herausforderungen, die für RBC/Zug-Kommunikationsketten relevant sind, aber nicht als konkreter RBC-Technologie-Release-Stand belegt sind. [9][10]

Missing information:
- Aktuelle normative Referenz zum RBC in ETCS/ERTMS (z. B. Subset-026, Baseline 3/4, Schnittstellen-/Nachrichtenspezifikationen wie Euroradio, RBC-RBC/IXL-Interfaces) inkl. Versions-/Änderungsstand ist nicht belegt.
- Konkreter Markt-/Produktstand heutiger RBC-Lösungen (Lieferanten, Releases, Funktionsumfang wie ATO over ETCS, Schnittstellenstandardisierung, Redundanz/Failover) ist nicht belegt.
- Nachweise zu Virtualisierung/Cloud-native/distributed RBC-Architekturen und deren Reifegrad/Zulassung sind nicht belegt.
- Aktueller Stand zu Safety Case/Zertifizierung (SIL4, EN 50126/50128/50129) für RBC in aktuellen Projekten ist nicht belegt; es gibt nur allgemeinen Standardverweis im Kontext Safety-Critical-Entwicklung.
- Spezifische RBC-bezogene Cybersecurity-Anforderungen/Standards (z. B. Euroradio-Weiterentwicklung, ETCS over IP, FRMCS-Migration) und deren Umsetzungsstand in RBCs sind nicht belegt.

Aufgestellte Hypothesen:
Hypothese 1: In ETCS/ERTMS Level 2 ist ein RBC typischerweise nur für einen begrenzten geografischen/streckenseitigen Zuständigkeitsbereich ausgelegt; dadurch sind Schnittstellen zu benachbarten RBCs sowie formalisierte Train-Hand-Over-Prozeduren erforderlich, um durchgehende Zugfahrten über RBC-Grenzen zu ermöglichen.
Reasoning: fit=0.65, semantic=inferential, sources=2, diversity=0.60, contradiction=False
Supporting evidence document IDs: [3], [7]

Hypothese 2: Stand der Technik (Implementierungsarchitektur) für RBC-Software umfasst eine modulare Prozess-Architektur mit (a) Application-Prozessen in einer Safe-Subset-C-Implementierung für externe Interaktionen (u.a. Zugfunk/Train interaction, Interlocking/IXL interaction, Operator) und (b) separaten Logic-Prozessen in einer applikationsspezifischen Logiksprache, die durch einen Logic Manager interpretiert und geschedult werden; zentrale Logikprozesse prüfen z.B. die Integrität der Movement Authority anhand Strecken-/Belegungsbedingungen aus dem Interlocking.
Reasoning: fit=0.58, semantic=tangential, sources=1, diversity=0.33, contradiction=False
Supporting evidence document IDs: [3]

Hypothese 3: RBC-Software wird in der Praxis gezielt performanzoptimiert (ohne unmittelbare Safety-Auswirkung), indem Prüfbedingungen in der Logik refaktoriert (Gruppierung/Umordnung) werden; solche Optimierungen können die Anzahl der vom RBC gleichzeitig handhabbaren Züge signifikant erhöhen (Größenordnung ~30% in einem industriellen Fallbeispiel).
Reasoning: fit=0.60, semantic=tangential, sources=2, diversity=0.50, contradiction=False
Supporting evidence document IDs: [5], [6]

Hypothese 4: Ein aktueller Entwicklungs- und Forschungsfokus für RBC-nahe Systeme liegt in der Absicherung und Leistungsfähigkeit der Zug-Funkkommunikation (insb. bei GSM-R): fehlende Ende-zu-Ende-Verschlüsselung und einseitige Authentisierung ermöglichen aktive Angriffe; zugleich werden Kommunikationsunterbrechungen und Latenzen als zentrales Performance-Problem der Train-Ground-Links diskutiert.
Reasoning: fit=0.45, semantic=tangential, sources=2, diversity=0.50, contradiction=False
Supporting evidence document IDs: [9], [8]
